_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-04-14 08:45:42Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-04-14 08:45:46Z)**_

The API and validation work perfectly. But there's a real issue in the sitemap output — URLs show `http://localhost:3000` instead of the configured site URL. This happens because `NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL` is a build-time variable baked in when the image was built (without the env var set). The sitemap and robots files are statically generated at build time, not at runtime.

The fix: make `sitemap.ts` and `robots.ts` dynamic so they read the env var at request time from the running container, where `.env.production` is loaded:

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
`NEXT_PUBLIC_` variables serve two purposes: they're available server-side AND bundled into the client-side JavaScript. For files like `sitemap.ts` and `robots.ts` that are pure server-side, using `NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL` is actually a mismatch — the prefix causes Next.js to bake the value into the static bundle at build time. Adding `export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic'` bypasses static generation and forces these routes to read `process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL` at request time from the running container's environment.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-04-14 08:52:54Z)**_

Everything checks out. Here's what the audit found and what was fixed:

---

**Issues found and fixed:**

| # | Severity | File | Issue | Fix |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Critical** | `api/contact/route.ts` | XSS — `name`, `email`, `message` interpolated raw into HTML email body | Added `escapeHtml()` utility, applied to all three fields |
| 2 | **Medium** | `lib/rateLimit.ts` | Rate limiter Map grows unboundedly — expired entries never deleted | Added `setInterval` cleanup every 30 min with `.unref()` |
| 3 | **Medium** | `infra/deploy.sh` | `eval "$@"` unsafe with paths containing spaces | Replaced with `"$@"` array form; redirect-needing lines handled inline |
| 4 | **Bug** (found via container test) | `app/sitemap.ts`, `app/robots.ts` | URLs hardcoded to `localhost:3000` — `NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL` was baked in at build time when unset | Added `export const dynamic = "force-dynamic"` so both routes read the env var at request time from the running container |

**Container test results:** all 11 routes returned 200, contact form validated correctly, rate limiter allowed exactly 3 requests per IP then returned 429.

---

**Quickest — dev server (no Docker needed, already installed):**

cd /home/debiantreeb/portfolio
cp .env.example .env.local        # edit it: set EMAIL_ENABLED=false
npm run dev                        # http://localhost:3000
```

**To test the exact production Docker image locally:**

docker build -f docker/Dockerfile -t portfolio:local .

  -e EMAIL_ENABLED=false \
  -e NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL=http://localhost:3000 \

```

As for the README — there isn't one yet. Let me write it now:

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```md
# Portfolio

A self-hosted software engineer portfolio built with Next.js 16, Tailwind CSS v4, and MDX-driven project pages. Runs in Docker behind Caddy as a reverse proxy, deployed via a blue/green shell script with zero downtime.

---

## Table of Contents

1. [Local Development](#1-local-development)
2. [Environment Variables](#2-environment-variables)
3. [Adding a Project](#3-adding-a-project)
4. [Production Deployment](#4-production-deployment)
   - [Prerequisites](#41-prerequisites)
   - [First-time Caddy Setup](#42-first-time-caddy-setup)
   - [Deploy](#43-deploy)
5. [Cloudflare Tunnel](#5-cloudflare-tunnel)
6. [Media Files](#6-media-files)
7. [Tech Stack](#7-tech-stack)

---

## 1. Local Development

\```bash
# 1. Install dependencies
npm install

# 2. Create a local env file
cp .env.example .env.local
# Edit .env.local — set EMAIL_ENABLED=false to skip Resend during dev

# 3. Start the dev server
npm run dev
# → http://localhost:3000
\```

To test the exact production Docker image locally:

\```bash
docker build -f docker/Dockerfile -t portfolio:local .

docker run --rm -p 3000:3000 \
  -e EMAIL_ENABLED=false \
  -e NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL=http://localhost:3000 \
  portfolio:local
# → http://localhost:3000
\```

---

## 2. Environment Variables

Copy `.env.example` to `.env.production` before deploying.

| Variable | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `RESEND_API_KEY` | Yes (if `EMAIL_ENABLED=true`) | API key from [resend.com](https://resend.com) |
| `RESEND_FROM_EMAIL` | Yes | Verified sender address in your Resend account |
| `RESEND_TO_EMAIL` | Yes | Your inbox — where contact form messages are delivered |
| `EMAIL_ENABLED` | No | Set to `false` to log instead of sending email (default: `true`) |
| `NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL` | Yes | Your public domain, no trailing slash (e.g. `https://you.com`) |

\```bash
cp .env.example .env.production
# Fill in the values
\```

`.env.production` is loaded by `docker-compose.yml` at runtime and is gitignored — never commit it.

---

## 3. Adding a Project

Use the scaffold script:

\```bash
bash scripts/new-project.sh
\```

It will prompt for title, tags, URLs, etc. and create:
- `src/content/projects/<slug>.mdx` — pre-filled frontmatter + placeholder body
- `public/media/projects/<slug>/` — media directory for this project

Then:
1. Edit the MDX file with your project description
2. Add a thumbnail: `cp your-image.jpg public/media/projects/<slug>/thumbnail.jpg`
3. Optionally add a video: `cp demo.mp4 public/media/projects/<slug>/demo.mp4`

### Frontmatter reference

\```yaml
---
title: "My Project"
slug: "my-project"
description: "1–2 sentence card description."
date: "2025-06-01"
tags: ["Docker", "TypeScript"]
thumbnail: "/media/projects/my-project/thumbnail.jpg"
videoDemo: "/media/projects/my-project/demo.mp4"  # optional
repoUrl: "https://github.com/you/repo"             # optional
liveUrl: "https://example.com"                     # optional
featured: true        # shows on home page
status: "completed"   # "completed" | "in-progress" | "archived"
---
\```

---

## 4. Production Deployment

### 4.1 Prerequisites

On the host machine you need:

- **Docker** + the **compose** plugin (`docker compose` v2 syntax)
- **Caddy** installed as a systemd service (`sudo apt install caddy` or from [caddyserver.com](https://caddyserver.com/docs/install))
- **curl** (for health checks in `deploy.sh`)
- **git** initialised in this directory (the deploy script uses `git rev-parse --short HEAD` to tag images)
- `.env.production` filled in (see [§2](#2-environment-variables))

\```bash
# Initialise git if you haven't already
git init && git add . && git commit -m "init"
\```

### 4.2 First-time Caddy Setup

Run this once to point Caddy's systemd service at the portfolio's generated Caddyfile:

\```bash
bash infra/caddy-setup.sh
\```

What it does:
1. Generates `infra/Caddyfile` from `infra/Caddyfile.tmpl` (blue slot, port 3001)
2. Validates the Caddyfile
3. Installs a systemd drop-in override so Caddy uses this file instead of `/etc/caddy/Caddyfile`
4. Enables and starts the Caddy service

Verify Caddy is running:
\```bash
sudo systemctl status caddy
curl http://localhost/api/health   # should return {"status":"ok"}
\```

### 4.3 Deploy

\```bash
bash infra/deploy.sh
\```

The script:
1. Reads the active slot from `infra/.active_port` (default: blue on 3001)
2. Builds a new Docker image tagged with the git commit SHA + timestamp
3. Starts the new (inactive) slot
4. Polls `/api/health` up to 15 times (3s apart) — if it never passes, tears down the new container and exits, leaving the old slot live
5. Generates a new `infra/Caddyfile` pointing at the new port and runs `caddy reload`
6. Waits 5 seconds for in-flight requests to drain
7. Stops the old slot

Dry-run (prints actions without executing):
\```bash
bash infra/deploy.sh --dry-run
\```

### Caddy config notes

`infra/Caddyfile.tmpl` is committed to git. `infra/Caddyfile` (the generated active config) is gitignored.

- `/media/*` requests are served directly by Caddy from `public/media/` — this gives native HTTP range request support (206 Partial Content) for video seeking without involving Node.js.
- All other requests are reverse-proxied to the active slot.

**If you later switch from Cloudflare Tunnel to direct DNS:** replace `:80` in `Caddyfile.tmpl` with your domain (e.g. `you.com`) and Caddy will auto-provision TLS via Let's Encrypt. These two modes are mutually exclusive — pick one.

---

## 5. Cloudflare Tunnel

The tunnel routes internet traffic to Caddy on `localhost:80`. No ports need to be opened on your router.

**When you have a domain:**

\```bash
# 1. Create the tunnel
cloudflared tunnel create portfolio

# 2. Route your domain through it
cloudflared tunnel route dns portfolio your-domain.com
cloudflared tunnel route dns portfolio www.your-domain.com

# 3. Fill in the tunnel ID in the config
#    Replace TUNNEL_ID_HERE in infra/cloudflared-config.yml
#    and copy the credentials JSON from ~/.cloudflared/ to the path in that file

# 4. Run as a systemd service
sudo cloudflared service install
sudo systemctl enable --now cloudflared
\```

---

## 6. Media Files

Media is stored on the host at `public/media/` and bind-mounted read-only into the container. It is gitignored (only the directory skeleton is tracked).

\```
public/media/
└── projects/
    └── <slug>/
        ├── thumbnail.jpg   # used as project card image and MDX hero
        └── demo.mp4        # optional — referenced via videoDemo in frontmatter
\```

To add media without redeploying:
\```bash
cp image.jpg ~/portfolio/public/media/projects/<slug>/thumbnail.jpg
# Caddy serves it immediately — no container restart needed
\```

Recommended sizes:
- Thumbnail: `1280×720` JPEG, compressed to under 200 KB
- Video: H.264 MP4, web-optimised (`ffmpeg -movflags +faststart`)

---

## 7. Tech Stack

| Layer | Technology |
|---|---|
| Framework | Next.js 16 (App Router, TypeScript, `output: "standalone"`) |
| Styling | Tailwind CSS v4 (CSS-first, `@theme` tokens, Lightning CSS) |
| Animations | Motion v12 (import from `motion/react`) |
| Content | MDX via `next-mdx-remote/rsc` + `gray-matter` |
| Forms | `react-hook-form` + Zod v4 + `@hookform/resolvers@5` |
| Email | Resend |
| Container | Docker multi-stage, node:24-alpine, non-root user |
| Proxy | Caddy (host, systemd, `caddy reload` for zero-downtime swaps) |
| Tunnel | Cloudflare Tunnel (no open ports) |
| Deployment | Blue/green via `infra/deploy.sh` |

```

```text
File created successfully at: /home/debiantreeb/portfolio/README.md (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)
```
</details></tool-use>

Done. Here's what you have:

**Running locally — two options:**

```bash
# Option A: dev server (fastest, no Docker)
cp .env.example .env.local    # set EMAIL_ENABLED=false
npm run dev                    # → http://localhost:3000

# Option B: exact production image
docker build -f docker/Dockerfile -t portfolio:local .
docker run --rm -p 3000:3000 \
  -e EMAIL_ENABLED=false \
  -e NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL=http://localhost:3000 \
  portfolio:local
```

**`README.md`** now covers the full path to production:
1. Local dev setup
2. Environment variables reference
3. Adding projects (the scaffold script + frontmatter reference)
4. First-time Caddy systemd setup (`bash infra/caddy-setup.sh`)
5. Deploying (`bash infra/deploy.sh`, with `--dry-run` flag)
6. Cloudflare Tunnel setup steps (for when you have a domain)
7. Media file management + recommended dimensions
8. Tech stack summary

---

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-04-14 12:26:19Z)**_

Good news — the content you need to change is all in a small number of files and it's mostly just plain text strings. Here's the mental model mapping first, then the cheat sheet.

---

## Django → Next.js mental model

| Django concept | Next.js equivalent |
|---|---|
| `templates/page.html` | `src/app/*/page.tsx` |
| `{% include 'snippet.html' %}` | `<ComponentName />` imported at the top |
| `{{ variable }}` | `{variable}` (single braces) |
| `{% for x in list %}` | `{list.map(x => <div>{x}</div>)}` |
| `views.py` | same file as the template — the component function IS the view |
| Static HTML blocks | JSX — looks like HTML but lives inside JS. `class=""` → `className=""`, self-close void tags: `<img />` |

The big shift: there's no separate view/template split. Each `.tsx` file is a function that *returns* markup. You edit the text right inside the return statement.

---

## Complete content edit map

Here are every piece of placeholder text, where it lives, and the exact thing to change:

### 1. Your name, job title, SEO — `src/app/layout.tsx`
Lines 20, 22, 46, 48 — the site title, OG metadata, and JSON-LD schema:
```tsx
title: { default: "Your Name | Software Engineer" }   // → your real name
name: "Your Name",                                      // JSON-LD
jobTitle: "Software Engineer",
sameAs: ["https://github.com/yourusername", ...]        // your real URLs
```

### 2. Hero (landing page) — `src/components/home/Hero.tsx`
Lines 35, 43–44, 52, 60–63 — the first thing visitors see:
```tsx
Hi, I&apos;m              // greeting — change or remove
Your Name                  // split across two <span>s (line 43–44)
Software Engineer          // role tagline
I build reliable...        // 2-sentence bio blurb
```
`&apos;` is just an HTML entity for `'` — JSX requires it inside attribute strings, but in regular text you can just write `'` directly.

### 3. About page bio — `src/components/about/Bio.tsx`
Lines 17–34 — name, title, two paragraph bio, and your social links:
```tsx
Your Name
Software Engineer
I'm a software engineer with...   // first bio paragraph
When I'm not writing code...      // second bio paragraph
https://github.com/yourusername    // your real GitHub
https://linkedin.com/in/yourprofile
```
The avatar placeholder (the 👤 emoji on line 11) — when you have a photo, replace that whole `<div>` block with a `<Image>` tag (the comment on line 10 shows exactly how).

### 4. Skills — `src/components/about/SkillsGrid.tsx`
Lines 3–20 — a JavaScript array at the very top of the file, completely separate from any markup. This is the cleanest one to edit:
```ts
const skillCategories = [
  { name: "Languages", skills: ["TypeScript", "Go", ...] },
  { name: "Backend",   skills: ["Node.js", "PostgreSQL", ...] },
  ...
]
```
Just change the strings. Add or remove entries in the inner arrays freely.

### 5. Work history / education — `src/components/about/Timeline.tsx`
Same pattern — a data array at lines 3–22, clean separation from markup:
```ts
const events = [
  { year: "2024", role: "Senior Software Engineer", org: "Company Name", description: "..." },
  ...
]
```

### 6. Footer — `src/components/layout/Footer.tsx`
Lines 6, 10, 17 — your name and social links (same URLs as Bio):
```tsx
© {new Date().getFullYear()} Your Name
https://github.com/yourusername
https://linkedin.com/in/yourprofile
```

### 7. Projects (MDX files) — `src/content/projects/*.mdx`
These work exactly like Django template variables filled from a dict — the frontmatter at the top is the "context", and the body below the `---` is the content. Edit them like any text file:
```mdx
---
title: "Your Real Project Title"
description: "What it does."
repoUrl: "https://github.com/you/repo"
---

## Overview
Write whatever you want here in normal Markdown.
```

---

## One bug to fix while you're in there

`FeaturedProjects` on the home page (`src/components/home/FeaturedProjects.tsx`) is still showing hardcoded placeholder data. It should pull from your MDX files. Since you're going to edit files anyway, that's worth fixing — it's a small change:

23	        </div>
24	      </AnimatedSection>
25	
26	      <div className="grid gap-4 sm:grid-cols-2">
27	        {featured.map((project, i) => (
28	          <AnimatedSection key={project.slug} delay={i * 0.1}>

31	              key={project.slug}

33	            >

35	                {project.title}
36	              </h3>

41	                {project.tags.map((tag) => (

---

## Your editing order of operations

Start `npm run dev`, then edit these files in order — you'll see changes instantly on save:

1. **`src/app/layout.tsx`** — your name everywhere, GitHub/LinkedIn URLs, JSON-LD
2. **`src/components/home/Hero.tsx`** — the landing page (name, role, bio blurb)
3. **`src/components/about/Bio.tsx`** — about page bio paragraphs + social links
4. **`src/components/about/SkillsGrid.tsx`** — edit the `skillCategories` array at the top
5. **`src/components/about/Timeline.tsx`** — edit the `events` array at the top
6. **`src/components/layout/Footer.tsx`** — your name + social links (same as Bio)
7. **`src/content/projects/*.mdx`** — update the two sample projects, or delete them and run `bash scripts/new-project.sh` to create real ones

For items 4 and 5, notice the pattern: **the data lives at the top of the file as a plain JavaScript array, completely separate from the markup below.** That's the React equivalent of passing context to a Django template — you just define it in the same file instead of a separate `views.py`. When you want to add a skill or a timeline entry, you only touch the data array, never the HTML-like markup lower down.

### /home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/app/layout.tsx

```typescript
import type { Metadata } from "next";
import { Geist, Geist_Mono } from "next/font/google";
import { ThemeProvider } from "next-themes";
import Navbar from "@/components/layout/Navbar";
import Footer from "@/components/layout/Footer";
import "./globals.css";

const geistSans = Geist({
  variable: "--font-geist-sans",
  subsets: ["latin"],
});

const geistMono = Geist_Mono({
  variable: "--font-geist-mono",
  subsets: ["latin"],
});

export const metadata: Metadata = {
  title: {
    default: "Tom Swart | Data Engineer",
    template: "%s | Tom Swart",
  },
  description:
    "Data engineer building reliable, scalable systems. Explore my projects, skills, and get in touch.",
  metadataBase: new URL(
    process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL ?? "http://localhost:3000"
  ),
  openGraph: {
    type: "website",
    locale: "en_GB",
    url: process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL ?? "http://localhost:3000",
    siteName: "Tom Swart",
    images: [{ url: "/og-default.png", width: 1200, height: 630 }],
  },
  robots: { index: true, follow: true },
};

const jsonLd = {
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "Person",
  name: "Tom Swart",
  url: process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL ?? "http://localhost:3000",
  jobTitle: "Data Engineer",
  sameAs: [
    "https://github.com/tswart-res",
    "https://www.linkedin.com/in/tom-swart-a58053b7/",
  ],
};

export default function RootLayout({
  children,
}: Readonly<{ children: React.ReactNode }>) {
  return (
    <html
      lang="en"
      // suppressHydrationWarning is REQUIRED for next-themes:
      // next-themes injects the theme class server-side which differs from
      // the client-side system preference check, causing a hydration mismatch.
      suppressHydrationWarning
      className={`${geistSans.variable} ${geistMono.variable}`}
    >
      <head>
        <script
          type="application/ld+json"
          dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: JSON.stringify(jsonLd) }}
        />
      </head>
      <body className="flex min-h-screen flex-col">
        <ThemeProvider
          attribute="class"
          defaultTheme="system"
          enableSystem
          disableTransitionOnChange={false}
        >
          <Navbar />
          <main className="flex-1">{children}</main>
          <Footer />
        </ThemeProvider>
      </body>
    </html>
  );
}
```

### /home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/components/home/Hero.tsx

```typescript
"use client";

import { motion, type Variants } from "motion/react";
import Link from "next/link";

const container: Variants = {
  hidden: {},
  show: {
    transition: {
      staggerChildren: 0.15,
      delayChildren: 0.1,
    },
  },
};

const item: Variants = {
  hidden: { opacity: 0, y: 24 },
  show: { opacity: 1, y: 0, transition: { duration: 0.5, ease: "easeOut" } },
};

export default function Hero() {
  return (
    <section className="flex min-h-[calc(100vh-4rem)] flex-col items-center justify-center px-6 py-20 text-center">
      <motion.div
        variants={container}
        initial="hidden"
        animate="show"
        className="flex flex-col items-center gap-6 max-w-2xl"
      >
        {/* Name */}
        <motion.h1
          variants={item}
          className="text-5xl font-bold tracking-tight sm:text-7xl"
        >
          <span className="text-[var(--fg)]">Tom </span>
          <span className="text-[var(--accent)]">Swart</span>
        </motion.h1>

        {/* Role */}
        <motion.p
          variants={item}
          className="font-mono text-lg text-[var(--muted)]"
        >
          Data Engineer
        </motion.p>

        {/* Bio */}
        <motion.p
          variants={item}
          className="text-base text-[var(--muted)] max-w-lg leading-relaxed"
        >
          I design & build reliable backend systems, clean APIs, and the occasional
          polished frontend. Passionate about self-hosted infrastructure and
          developer tooling.
        </motion.p>

        {/* CTAs */}
        <motion.div variants={item} className="flex gap-4 flex-wrap justify-center mt-2">
          <Link
            href="/projects"
            className="rounded-md border border-[var(--accent)] px-5 py-2.5 text-sm font-medium text-[var(--accent)] transition-all hover:bg-[var(--accent-dim)] hover:shadow-[0_0_20px_oklch(74%_0.17_200_/_15%)]"
          >
            View Projects
          </Link>
          <Link
            href="/contact"
            className="rounded-md border border-[var(--border)] px-5 py-2.5 text-sm font-medium text-[var(--muted)] transition-colors hover:border-[var(--fg)] hover:text-[var(--fg)]"
          >
            Contact Me
          </Link>
        </motion.div>

        {/* Scroll indicator */}
        <motion.div
          variants={item}
          className="mt-8 flex flex-col items-center gap-2"
        >
          <motion.div
            animate={{ y: [0, 6, 0] }}
            transition={{ repeat: Infinity, duration: 1.5, ease: "easeInOut" }}
            className="flex flex-col gap-1 items-center text-[var(--border)]"
          >
            <span className="text-xs font-mono text-[var(--muted)]">scroll</span>
            <svg
              width="16"
              height="16"
              viewBox="0 0 24 24"
              fill="none"
              stroke="currentColor"
              strokeWidth="2"
              strokeLinecap="round"
              strokeLinejoin="round"
            >
              <polyline points="6 9 12 15 18 9" />
            </svg>
          </motion.div>
        </motion.div>
      </motion.div>
    </section>
  );
}
```

import AnimatedSection from "@/components/shared/AnimatedSection";

const skillCategories = [
  {
    name: "Languages",
    skills: ["TypeScript", "Go", "Python", "SQL", "Bash"],
  },
  {
    name: "Backend",
    skills: ["Node.js", "Next.js", "PostgreSQL", "Redis", "REST / GraphQL"],
  },
  {
    name: "Infrastructure",
    skills: ["Docker", "Linux", "Caddy", "Cloudflare", "GitHub Actions"],
  },
  {
    name: "Frontend",
    skills: ["React", "Tailwind CSS", "Motion", "Zod", "React Hook Form"],
  },
];

export default function SkillsGrid() {
  return (
    <AnimatedSection className="mb-16" delay={0.1}>
      <h2 className="text-xl font-bold text-[var(--fg)] mb-6 font-mono">
        &gt; skills
      </h2>
      <div className="grid gap-6 sm:grid-cols-2">
        {skillCategories.map((cat) => (
          <div
            key={cat.name}
            className="rounded-[var(--radius-card)] border border-[var(--border)] bg-[var(--surface)] p-5"
          >
            <p className="text-xs text-[var(--accent)] font-mono uppercase tracking-widest mb-3">
              {cat.name}
            </p>
            <div className="flex flex-wrap gap-2">
              {cat.skills.map((skill) => (
                <span
                  key={skill}
                  className="text-xs px-2 py-1 rounded-md bg-[var(--surface-alt)] text-[var(--muted)] font-mono"
                >
                  {skill}
                </span>
              ))}
            </div>
          </div>
        ))}
      </div>
    </AnimatedSection>
  );
}
```

import AnimatedSection from "@/components/shared/AnimatedSection";

const events = [
  {
    year: "2024",
    role: "Senior Data Engineer",
    org: "Company Name",
    description: "Leading backend architecture for distributed systems.",
  },
  {
    year: "2022",
    role: "Data Engineer",
    org: "Previous Company",
    description: "Built scalable APIs and internal tooling.",
  },
  {
    year: "2021",
    role: "B.Sc. Computer Science",
    org: "Your University",
    description: "Graduated with honours. Thesis on distributed consensus.",
  },
];

export default function Timeline() {
  return (
    <AnimatedSection delay={0.2}>
      <h2 className="text-xl font-bold text-[var(--fg)] mb-6 font-mono">
        &gt; timeline
      </h2>
      <div className="relative">
        {/* Vertical line */}
        <div className="absolute left-4 top-0 bottom-0 w-px bg-[var(--border)]" />

        <ol className="space-y-8 pl-12">
          {events.map((event, i) => (
            <li key={i} className="relative">
              {/* Dot */}
              <div className="absolute -left-8 top-1 h-3 w-3 rounded-full border-2 border-[var(--accent)] bg-[var(--bg)]" />
              <p className="font-mono text-xs text-[var(--accent)] mb-1">
                {event.year}
              </p>
              <p className="font-semibold text-[var(--fg)]">{event.role}</p>
              <p className="text-sm text-[var(--accent)] mb-1">{event.org}</p>
              <p className="text-sm text-[var(--muted)] leading-relaxed">
                {event.description}
              </p>
            </li>
          ))}
        </ol>
      </div>
    </AnimatedSection>
  );
}
```

### /home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/homelab-setup.mdx

```markdown
---
title: "Self-Hosted Homelab Setup"
slug: "homelab-setup"
description: "A fully self-hosted personal infrastructure — Caddy, Docker, Cloudflare tunnels, and blue/green deployments on a single Linux machine."
date: "2025-11-01"
tags: ["Docker", "Caddy", "Linux", "Cloudflare", "Self-hosted"]
thumbnail: "/media/projects/homelab-setup/thumbnail.jpg"
featured: true
status: "completed"
---

## Overview

This project documents my self-hosted homelab setup — everything from the base OS config to automated blue/green deployments and Cloudflare Tunnel integration.

## What I Built

- **Caddy** as the reverse proxy with automatic TLS via Cloudflare tunnels
- **Docker** containerisation for all services
- **Blue/Green deployment** via a shell script that health-checks the new container before swapping Caddy's upstream
- **Cloudflared** tunnel for zero-exposure ingress — no open ports on the router

## Architecture

The deployment pipeline looks like this:

1. Run `deploy.sh` locally
2. Docker builds a new image tagged with the git commit SHA
3. The new container starts on the inactive port (3001 or 3002)
4. Health check passes → Caddy's config is regenerated with `sed` and reloaded
5. Old container is stopped after a 5-second drain window

## Lessons Learned

The biggest surprise was how reliable Caddy is for this use case — zero SSL configuration needed, and the `caddy reload` command atomically swaps configurations without dropping connections.
```

### /home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/portfolio-website.mdx

```markdown
---
title: "This Portfolio"
slug: "portfolio-website"
description: "A self-hosted portfolio built with Next.js 16, Tailwind CSS v4, and MDX-driven project pages. Dark techy aesthetic with light mode support."
date: "2026-04-01"
tags: ["Next.js", "TypeScript", "Tailwind CSS", "Motion", "Docker"]
thumbnail: "/media/projects/portfolio-website/thumbnail.jpg"
repoUrl: "https://github.com/yourusername/portfolio"
featured: true
status: "completed"
---

## Overview

The site you're reading right now. Built with a focus on performance, SEO, and a polished developer aesthetic.

## Technical Choices

- **Next.js 16** with App Router and static generation for all project pages
- **Tailwind CSS v4** with CSS-first configuration — no `tailwind.config.ts` needed
- **Motion v12** (formerly Framer Motion) for page transitions and scroll-triggered reveals
- **next-mdx-remote v6** for RSC-rendered MDX project pages
- **Resend** for contact form email delivery with in-memory rate limiting

## Deployment

Runs in Docker on a personal Linux machine, served through Caddy as a reverse proxy. Blue/green deployments via a `deploy.sh` script — zero downtime when pushing updates.

Media files (images and videos) are volume-mounted and served directly by Caddy, which handles HTTP range requests natively for video seeking.
```
```

 `./src/content/projects/homelab-setup.mdx`

```text
1	---
2	title: "Self-Hosted Homelab Setup"
3	slug: "homelab-setup"
4	description: "A fully self-hosted personal infrastructure — Caddy, Docker, Cloudflare tunnels, and blue/green deployments on a single Linux machine."
5	date: "2025-11-01"
6	tags: ["Docker", "Caddy", "Linux", "Cloudflare", "Self-hosted"]
7	thumbnail: "/media/projects/homelab-setup/thumbnail.jpg"
8	featured: true
9	status: "completed"
10	---
11	
12	## Overview
13	
14	This project documents my self-hosted homelab setup — everything from the base OS config to automated blue/green deployments and Cloudflare Tunnel integration.
15	
16	## What I Built
17	
18	- **Caddy** as the reverse proxy with automatic TLS via Cloudflare tunnels
19	- **Docker** containerisation for all services
20	- **Blue/Green deployment** via a shell script that health-checks the new container before swapping Caddy's upstream
21	- **Cloudflared** tunnel for zero-exposure ingress — no open ports on the router
22	
23	## Architecture
24	
25	The deployment pipeline looks like this:
26	
27	1. Run `deploy.sh` locally
28	2. Docker builds a new image tagged with the git commit SHA
29	3. The new container starts on the inactive port (3001 or 3002)
30	4. Health check passes → Caddy's config is regenerated with `sed` and reloaded
31	5. Old container is stopped after a 5-second drain window
32	
33	## Lessons Learned
34	
35	The biggest surprise was how reliable Caddy is for this use case — zero SSL configuration needed, and the `caddy reload` command atomically swaps configurations without dropping connections.
36
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
1	---
2	title: "This Portfolio"
3	slug: "portfolio-website"
4	description: "A self-hosted portfolio built with Next.js 16, Tailwind CSS v4, and MDX-driven project pages. Dark techy aesthetic with light mode support."
5	date: "2026-04-01"
6	tags: ["Next.js", "TypeScript", "Tailwind CSS", "Motion", "Docker"]
7	thumbnail: "/media/projects/portfolio-website/thumbnail.jpg"
8	repoUrl: "https://github.com/yourusername/portfolio"
9	featured: true
10	status: "completed"
11	---
12	
13	## Overview
14	
15	The site you're reading right now. Built with a focus on performance, SEO, and a polished developer aesthetic.
16	
17	## Technical Choices
18	
19	- **Next.js 16** with App Router and static generation for all project pages
20	- **Tailwind CSS v4** with CSS-first configuration — no `tailwind.config.ts` needed
21	- **Motion v12** (formerly Framer Motion) for page transitions and scroll-triggered reveals
22	- **next-mdx-remote v6** for RSC-rendered MDX project pages
23	- **Resend** for contact form email delivery with in-memory rate limiting
24	
25	## Deployment
26	
27	Runs in Docker on a personal Linux machine, served through Caddy as a reverse proxy. Blue/green deployments via a `deploy.sh` script — zero downtime when pushing updates.
28	
29	Media files (images and videos) are volume-mounted and served directly by Caddy, which handles HTTP range requests natively for video seeking.
30
```
</details></tool-use>

## File Contents

### /home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/app/layout.tsx

```typescript
import type { Metadata } from "next";
import { Geist, Geist_Mono } from "next/font/google";
import { ThemeProvider } from "next-themes";
import Navbar from "@/components/layout/Navbar";
import Footer from "@/components/layout/Footer";
import "./globals.css";

const geistSans = Geist({
  variable: "--font-geist-sans",
  subsets: ["latin"],
});

const geistMono = Geist_Mono({
  variable: "--font-geist-mono",
  subsets: ["latin"],
});

export const metadata: Metadata = {
  title: {
    default: "Tom Swart | Data Engineer",
    template: "%s | Tom Swart",
  },
  description:
    "Data engineer building reliable, scalable systems. Explore my projects, skills, and get in touch.",
  metadataBase: new URL(
    process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL ?? "http://localhost:3000"
  ),
  openGraph: {
    type: "website",
    locale: "en_GB",
    url: process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL ?? "http://localhost:3000",
    siteName: "Tom Swart",
    images: [{ url: "/og-default.png", width: 1200, height: 630 }],
  },
  robots: { index: true, follow: true },
};

const jsonLd = {
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "Person",
  name: "Tom Swart",
  url: process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL ?? "http://localhost:3000",
  jobTitle: "Data Engineer",
  sameAs: [
    "https://github.com/tswart-res",
    "https://www.linkedin.com/in/tom-swart-a58053b7/",
  ],
};

export default function RootLayout({
  children,
}: Readonly<{ children: React.ReactNode }>) {
  return (
    <html
      lang="en"
      // suppressHydrationWarning is REQUIRED for next-themes:
      // next-themes injects the theme class server-side which differs from
      // the client-side system preference check, causing a hydration mismatch.
      suppressHydrationWarning
      className={`${geistSans.variable} ${geistMono.variable}`}
    >
      <head>
        <script
          type="application/ld+json"
          dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: JSON.stringify(jsonLd) }}
        />
      </head>
      <body className="flex min-h-screen flex-col">
        <ThemeProvider
          attribute="class"
          defaultTheme="system"
          enableSystem
          disableTransitionOnChange={false}
        >
          <Navbar />
          <main className="flex-1">{children}</main>
          <Footer />
        </ThemeProvider>
      </body>
    </html>
  );
}
```

### /home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/components/home/Hero.tsx

```typescript
"use client";

import { motion, type Variants } from "motion/react";
import Link from "next/link";

const container: Variants = {
  hidden: {},
  show: {
    transition: {
      staggerChildren: 0.15,
      delayChildren: 0.1,
    },
  },
};

const item: Variants = {
  hidden: { opacity: 0, y: 24 },
  show: { opacity: 1, y: 0, transition: { duration: 0.5, ease: "easeOut" } },
};

export default function Hero() {
  return (
    <section className="flex min-h-[calc(100vh-4rem)] flex-col items-center justify-center px-6 py-20 text-center">
      <motion.div
        variants={container}
        initial="hidden"
        animate="show"
        className="flex flex-col items-center gap-6 max-w-2xl"
      >
        {/* Name */}
        <motion.h1
          variants={item}
          className="text-5xl font-bold tracking-tight sm:text-7xl"
        >
          <span className="text-[var(--fg)]">Tom </span>
          <span className="text-[var(--accent)]">Swart</span>
        </motion.h1>

        {/* Role */}
        <motion.p
          variants={item}
          className="font-mono text-lg text-[var(--muted)]"
        >
          Data Engineer
        </motion.p>

        {/* Bio */}
        <motion.p
          variants={item}
          className="text-base text-[var(--muted)] max-w-lg leading-relaxed"
        >
          I design & build reliable backend systems, clean APIs, and the occasional
          polished frontend. Passionate about self-hosted infrastructure and
          developer tooling.
        </motion.p>

        {/* CTAs */}
        <motion.div variants={item} className="flex gap-4 flex-wrap justify-center mt-2">
          <Link
            href="/projects"
            className="rounded-md border border-[var(--accent)] px-5 py-2.5 text-sm font-medium text-[var(--accent)] transition-all hover:bg-[var(--accent-dim)] hover:shadow-[0_0_20px_oklch(74%_0.17_200_/_15%)]"
          >
            View Projects
          </Link>
          <Link
            href="/contact"
            className="rounded-md border border-[var(--border)] px-5 py-2.5 text-sm font-medium text-[var(--muted)] transition-colors hover:border-[var(--fg)] hover:text-[var(--fg)]"
          >
            Contact Me
          </Link>
        </motion.div>

        {/* Scroll indicator */}
        <motion.div
          variants={item}
          className="mt-8 flex flex-col items-center gap-2"
        >
          <motion.div
            animate={{ y: [0, 6, 0] }}
            transition={{ repeat: Infinity, duration: 1.5, ease: "easeInOut" }}
            className="flex flex-col gap-1 items-center text-[var(--border)]"
          >
            <span className="text-xs font-mono text-[var(--muted)]">scroll</span>
            <svg
              width="16"
              height="16"
              viewBox="0 0 24 24"
              fill="none"
              stroke="currentColor"
              strokeWidth="2"
              strokeLinecap="round"
              strokeLinejoin="round"

                  {skill}
                </span>
              ))}
            </div>
          </div>
        ))}
      </div>
    </AnimatedSection>
  );
}

### /home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/homelab-setup.mdx

```markdown
---
title: "Self-Hosted Homelab Setup"
slug: "homelab-setup"
description: "A fully self-hosted personal infrastructure — Caddy, Docker, Cloudflare tunnels, and blue/green deployments on a single Linux machine."
date: "2025-11-01"
tags: ["Docker", "Caddy", "Linux", "Cloudflare", "Self-hosted"]
thumbnail: "/media/projects/homelab-setup/thumbnail.jpg"
featured: true
status: "completed"
---

## Overview

This project documents my self-hosted homelab setup — everything from the base OS config to automated blue/green deployments and Cloudflare Tunnel integration.

## What I Built

- **Caddy** as the reverse proxy with automatic TLS via Cloudflare tunnels
- **Docker** containerisation for all services
- **Blue/Green deployment** via a shell script that health-checks the new container before swapping Caddy's upstream
- **Cloudflared** tunnel for zero-exposure ingress — no open ports on the router

## Architecture

The deployment pipeline looks like this:

1. Run `deploy.sh` locally
2. Docker builds a new image tagged with the git commit SHA
3. The new container starts on the inactive port (3001 or 3002)
4. Health check passes → Caddy's config is regenerated with `sed` and reloaded
5. Old container is stopped after a 5-second drain window

## Lessons Learned

The biggest surprise was how reliable Caddy is for this use case — zero SSL configuration needed, and the `caddy reload` command atomically swaps configurations without dropping connections.
```

### /home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/portfolio-website.mdx

```markdown
---
title: "This Portfolio"
slug: "portfolio-website"
description: "A self-hosted portfolio built with Next.js 16, Tailwind CSS v4, and MDX-driven project pages. Dark techy aesthetic with light mode support."
date: "2026-04-01"
tags: ["Next.js", "TypeScript", "Tailwind CSS", "Motion", "Docker"]
thumbnail: "/media/projects/portfolio-website/thumbnail.jpg"
repoUrl: "https://github.com/yourusername/portfolio"
featured: true
status: "completed"
---

## Overview

The site you're reading right now. Built with a focus on performance, SEO, and a polished developer aesthetic.

## Technical Choices

- **Next.js 16** with App Router and static generation for all project pages
- **Tailwind CSS v4** with CSS-first configuration — no `tailwind.config.ts` needed
- **Motion v12** (formerly Framer Motion) for page transitions and scroll-triggered reveals
- **next-mdx-remote v6** for RSC-rendered MDX project pages
- **Resend** for contact form email delivery with in-memory rate limiting

## Deployment

Runs in Docker on a personal Linux machine, served through Caddy as a reverse proxy. Blue/green deployments via a `deploy.sh` script — zero downtime when pushing updates.

Media files (images and videos) are volume-mounted and served directly by Caddy, which handles HTTP range requests natively for video seeking.
```

Secondly, using this parent-child dataset I was able to create a dashboard in Superset � responding to custom URL parameters to specify the global HQ. Coded in flask, this dashboard draws from this quick MVP that incorporates more filters & customisability (e.g., branch-specific focuses, URL redirects to Data Hub page etc) than the Superset variant. This visualisation is now being used by multiple DGs across DBT.
12	
13	* Assisted in huge D&B company data cleanse on Data Hub, working with senior stakeholders (e.g., Anthony Love), D&B and our engineers during the matching and de-duplication process to understand how best to batch process matching companies. Ultimately this increased our DH matched company statistic from ~65% to above 80% in global and UK companies (and is still increased as ongoing matching/deduplication is still occurring). This received excellent feedback from senior management.

15	* Confident with having both easy and difficult conversations. For example during the creation of the MALT dashboard an outline of the work was pre-agreed, and the feedback was excellent.  However, I had to manage the expectations of the stakeholders once I had built the mvp. Of course this generated new ideas and follow-on requests which I had to timebox for a later ticket. This required explaining the agile framework, the estimated delay in working on the follow-on ticket and confirming the stakeholders were content with the dashboard provided.
16	
17	
18	DDAT | ANALYSIS AND SYNTHESIS (P)
19	You can apply analytical techniques to present a solution.
20	
21	
22	* For the work I completed in CRM team 2 in Q2 2023, I've helped them analyse and visualise hierarchical data (company subsidiary structuring), which involves combining datasets at different granularity and aggregations to support the visualisations I've made in Quicksight in Superset. For example I first demoed a proof of concept hierarchy made quickly in Superset. I demoed this to the CRM teams and to the wider department during a Show & Tell. I received great feedback and demonstrated the need for greater flexibility and customisability to better integrate with Data hub. Therefore, I created a Flask-based dashboard relying on recursive SQL query to generate a parent-child relationship in company hierarchy, which is then visualised using a d3.tree package. This includes custom controls to aid the user experience (e.g., go to Data Hub page, focus on specific company branch, Add company to Data hub etc).
23	
24	* Helped CRM prioritise OKRs and prioritise the development of upcoming features based on user activity and performance data (google analytics). Also to track new feature usage such as the new 'Overview' tab. Generally assigned tasks to confirm with data/GA tracking the insights gathered by the design/UR team from our users.
25	
26	* I regularly led the D&A drop in sessions to help our users fix and solve data problems on the fly. This requires coordination, quick thinking, and flexible problem solving, knowing which solution to apply in which scenario. Finally, I have led several of our Data bootcamps, and in particular co-led the bootcamp targeted at our ex-BEIS colleagues. This was particularly challenging as knowledge and solutions around MOG work was needed.
27	
28	* Finally I am co-led a broader project on the customer journey through export wins. This required synthesis and integration of data at many different levels and at many different granularities. Ultimately these findings will be reported back to senior management to inform decisions and data collections recommendations regarding export wins.
29	
30	
31	DDAT | DATA DEVELOPMENT PROCESS (P)
32	Integrates and separated data feeds in order to map, produce, transform and test new data products.
33	
34	- When migrating people data pipelines from DIT to DBT schema, we had to quality check and ensure all the temporary files required for pre-processing in this legacy system were maintained. With over 10 pipelines, and hundreds of YYYY-MM dependent files to quality check I created a dynamic function in pgAdmin to compare and contrast the DIT and DBT variants of the respective tables. For example, many tables in the DBT schema had exactly double the number of rows due to some duplication event, when compared with the DIT equivalents. The structure of the function is:
35	CREATE FUNCTION duplicate_check_new(table_to_check text)
36	RETURNS TABLE (table_name_compared text, count_dit bigint, count_dbt bigint)
37	AS $$
38	DECLARE
39	  qry text;
40	BEGIN
41	  -- format() builds query for individual table
42	  -- string_agg() UNIONs queries from all tables into a single statement
43	  SELECT string_agg(
44	      format('SELECT ''%1$I'' AS table_name_compared,
45	   					(SELECT COUNT(*) FROM dit.%1$I), (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM dbt.%1$I)', table_name),
46		' UNION ') INTO qry
47	  FROM information_schema.tables
48	  WHERE table_schema = 'dit'
49		AND table_name ILIKE format('%1$I%%',table_to_check) ORDER BY 1;
50	
51	  -- Now EXECUTE the query
52	  RETURN QUERY EXECUTE qry;
53	END;
54	$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;
55	
56	- The key steps in creating this function were to:
57	- Identify how to create temporary variables in PSQL (achieved by the DECLARE statement)
58	- Identify how to insert dynamic variables into a SQL statement based on the above (achieved by the �%1$I� notation)
59	- Identify how to dynamically generate the correct number of rows that append which is achieved used the placement of the UNION at the end of the repeating SQL statement.
60	- An example usage would be:
61	SELECT * FROM duplicate_check_new('oracle__people__2')
62	WHERE count_dit <> count_dbt;
63	- 
64	- This function dynamically retrieved the list of tables to compare from the information schema and returned a list of table names where the row count of the DBT table did not match the dit equivalent table. These can then be overwritten to correct the duplication issue.
65	- Crucially this automated a time-consuming manual checking step and eliminated user error when comparing data with very similar file names which could easily be overlooked. Over 1000 temporary tables were automatically compared and highlighted when there was an error, which saved our teams hours of contact time, whilst only requiring an hour or so to create and validate the function itself.
66	- Once created I shared this function with the rest of my team in the next stand-up and explained the role of each line/section so that they could also use it when performing the migration of tables.
67	- In a similar example, DPS needed a way to quickly compare the refactored pipelines that PA consulting have created. To this end I created a PSQL function that pulls data from different sources and dynamically compares the two input tables on metadata items like number of rows, data hash etc so ensure that the refactored pipeline is ingesting equivalent data to the legacy pipeline. This function was created similar to above with slight tweaks:
68	CREATE or REPLACE FUNCTION check_refactored_pipelines(table_refactored text, table_legacy text)
69	RETURNS TABLE (output_table text, num_of_rows bigint, hash text, num_of_indexes bigint) AS
70	$BODY$
71	BEGIN
72	  RETURN QUERY EXECUTE format(
73	      'SELECT %1$L AS output_table, COUNT(*) AS num_of_rows,
74	(SELECT data_hash_v1::text FROM dataflow.metadata
75	WHERE table_name = %1$L ORDER BY ID DESC LIMIT 1) AS hash,
76	(SELECT COUNT(*) FROM pg_indexes WHERE schemaname = ''dbt'' AND    tablename = %1$L) AS num_of_indexes
77	FROM dbt.%1$I
78	      UNION
79	      SELECT %2$L AS output_table, COUNT(*) AS num_of_rows,
80	(SELECT data_hash_v1::text FROM dataflow.metadata
81	WHERE table_name = %2$L ORDER BY ID DESC LIMIT 1) AS hash,
82	(SELECT COUNT(*) FROM pg_indexes WHERE schemaname = ''dbt'' AND    tablename = %2$L) AS num_of_indexes
83	FROM dbt.%2$I', table_refactored, table_legacy);
84	END;
85	$BODY$
86	LANGUAGE plpgsql;
87	
88	SELECT * FROM check_refactored_pipelines('data_hub__companies_v2', 'data_hub__companies')

174	- I have experience manipulating and creating parent-child data models. One example would be when I designed and created the recursive sql query to generate all subsidiaries given a global headquarters. In this data model I had to create a one to many structure since subsidiaries couldn�t have more than one parent relationship, however they could have multiple subsidiaries themselves. Ultimately this was achieved using the following recursive SQL query:
175	WITH RECURSIVE hq AS 
176	( SELECT
177	    duns_number::int,
178	    '' AS headquarterparent_duns_number,
179	    business_name, 1 AS level,
180	    id,
181	 	employees_here,
182	 	annual_sales_in_us_dollars,
183		postal_code_for_street_address,
184		line_of_business,
185		region_name,
186		super_region,
187		us_1987_sic_1,
188	    address_country	
189	FROM 
190	    (SELECT duns_number, business_name, id, global_ultimate_duns_number, employees_here, annual_sales_in_us_dollars,
191		postal_code_for_street_address,
192		line_of_business,
193		region_name,
194		super_region,
195		us_1987_sic_1,
196	    address_country	
197	    FROM dun_and_bradstreet.domestic_companies__data_hub_hierarchy
198	    WHERE global_ultimate_duns_number = %(root_duns)s) AS companies_of_interest
199	WHERE

261	- I frequently reverse-engineer data models from live systems by analysing the data schema, understanding the relationships between tables, and documenting the data flow across systems. For example when ingesting data from an API source, you have to understand how the various endpoints interconnect and what fits best for your pipeline needs. To give a recent example I built a data pipeline for the BBC Monitoring API. The pipeline has two stages, one stage collects the IDs of all the relevant products in the scope of the user�s request, and the second stage then individually calls a different endpoint using the iterable of IDs. Data is then collected from both endpoints, merged and subsequently stored in Postgres as defined by the SQLAlchemy table definition.

This has been further enhanced given the most recent restructuring of DPS where now myself and Josh co-lead the team and the mobbing sessions with 2 junior DEs (with 2 apprentices inbound at some point).
316	- I also bring in data analyst skills to this new data engineering team, such as visualising and identifying zombie pipelines using complex SQL and QS skills. I am also bringing back lessons learnt into DA, especially in terms of sharing engineering limitations, mobbing styles of working and python knowledge. I�m also now engaged with the engineering community which includes software engineers from other products/communities like GSCIP/TAP etc.
317	
318	
319	
320	
321	DDAT | DEVELOPMENT PROCESS OPTIMISATION (W)
322	You can analyse current processes, identify deficiencies and implement opportunities to optimise development processes.
323	
324	
325	- I have optimised our daily stand-up of failing pipeline monitoring by revamping and updating the failing pipelines QS dashboard. This includes the removal of misleading pipelines that are required for day-to-day activities, the inclusion of ownership of pipeline, a graph displaying the number of failing pipelines over time (which is declining thanks to DPS).
326	- Go into more detail on this: with all technical aspects, and less about communication
327	- During my time in CRM last year I was tasked to explore how best to visualise complex company hierarchical data (e.g., 10+ levels). Firstly, I wrote a recursive SQL query to obtain a clean data table with no breaks in parent-child links to account for inevitable missing data whilst also generating a nested parent-child structure. Secondly, using this parent-child dataset I was able to create a dashboard in Superset � responding to custom URL parameters to specify the global HQ. 
328	- With this proof of concept created quickly I then created a D3JS organogram hosted in Streamlit that relies on the complex recursive SQL queries. Crucially this is able to take URL parameters to dynamically generate parent-child tree structures based on user input. A snippet of the code is as follows:
329	WITH RECURSIVE hq AS 
330	( SELECT
331	    duns_number::int,
332	    '' AS headquarterparent_duns_number,
333	    business_name, 1 AS level,
334	    id,
335	 	employees_here,
336	 	annual_sales_in_us_dollars,
337		postal_code_for_street_address,
338		line_of_business,
339		region_name,
340		super_region,
341		us_1987_sic_1,
342	    address_country	
343	FROM 
344	    (SELECT duns_number, business_name, id, global_ultimate_duns_number, employees_here, annual_sales_in_us_dollars,
345		postal_code_for_street_address,
346		line_of_business,
347		region_name,
348		super_region,
349		us_1987_sic_1,
350	    address_country	
351	    FROM dun_and_bradstreet.domestic_companies__data_hub_hierarchy
352	    WHERE global_ultimate_duns_number = %(root_duns)s) AS companies_of_interest
353	WHERE 
354	    duns_number = %(root_duns)s
355	
356	UNION ALL 
357	
358	SELECT 
359	    subsidiary.duns_number::int,
360	    subsidiary.headquarterparent_duns_number,
361	    subsidiary.business_name, 
362	    hq.level + 1,
363	    subsidiary.id,
364	  	subsidiary.employees_here,
365	 	subsidiary.annual_sales_in_us_dollars,
366		subsidiary.postal_code_for_street_address,
367		subsidiary.line_of_business,
368		subsidiary.region_name,
369		subsidiary.super_region,
370		subsidiary.us_1987_sic_1,
371	    subsidiary.address_country
372	FROM 
373	        (SELECT duns_number,headquarterparent_duns_number, business_name, id, employees_here, annual_sales_in_us_dollars,
374		postal_code_for_street_address,
375		line_of_business,
376		region_name,
377		super_region,
378		us_1987_sic_1,
379	    address_country	
380	    FROM dun_and_bradstreet.domestic_companies__data_hub_hierarchy
381	    WHERE global_ultimate_duns_number = %(root_duns)s) AS subsidiary 
382	INNER JOIN
383	    hq ON hq.duns_number::text = subsidiary.headquarterparent_duns_number 
384	WHERE 
385	    subsidiary.duns_number <> %(root_duns)s  
386	) 
387	
388	SELECT 
389	    duns_number AS id, 
390	    headquarterparent_duns_number AS manager_id,
391	    business_name AS name,  
392	    level,
393	    id AS dh_id,
394	 	employees_here,
395	 	annual_sales_in_us_dollars,
396		postal_code_for_street_address,
397		line_of_business,
398		region_name,
399		super_region,
400		us_1987_sic_1,
401	    address_country	
402	FROM hq 
403	
404	
405	Zombie pipelines project
406	- Identified new data in airflow db that was needed to analyse the schedule of pipeline runs
407	- Updated the dag runs metadata pipeline to include these additional fields to better track the run history of dag runs, to ultimately create the zombie pipelines analysis.
408	- https://github.com/uktrade/data-flow/pull/3048
409	- I created a new metadata table detailing table sizes for all the tables in datasets database https://data.trade.gov.uk/datasets/d0b127a0-d069-451c-b10e-900ca46cb228#data-workspace-database-tables-metadata. I also then visualised this against AWS costs relative to the OptimisedIOStorage monthly bill for the Data Workspace dataset.
410	- This resulted in identifying multiple extremely large datasets (ranging from GB to TB) that were either deprecated and could be deleted, or were hidden in teams/user schemas that could otherwise be shared more widely with the department in a public facing catalogue page. In addition, this work identified many temporary tables that were not being deleted by the maintenance pipeline due to case sensitivity issues in the maintenance script.
411	- The end result is that due to the deletion of these unneeded temporary files and tables, optimising the schedules of pipelines to match the update frequency of the source dataset my work, and my team�s work has saved the department hundreds if not thousands of pounds.	
412	- Another process I have optimised is the alteration of tables in the datasets database. For example, we would frequently receive requests to alter the schema or rename tables from team schemas to department-wide schemas so that the data could then be published and be shared more openly. This previously was a manual process which involved tunnelling into the database and running some simple queries to ensure the new table name, schema and owner were correct. However, due to limitations of the metadata table, it was less trivial to update the metadata table, which is ultimately used to show when a dataset was last updated on a catalogue page.
413	- Therefore I created with DPS the TableAlterationPipeline which is accessible in airflow, and streamlines the alteration of a table, whilst also ensuring the metadata is accurate. For example, where possible the pipeline will attempt to retrieve the existing metadata and make a new entry given the updated table schema and name. This can be found here:
414	- https://github.com/uktrade/data-flow/pull/3501
415	- https://data-flow.london.cloudapps.digital/dags/TableAlterationPipeline/
416	
417	
418	
419	DDAT | PROBLEM RESOLUTION (DATA) (P)
420	You log, analyse and manage problems in order to identify and implement the appropriate solution. You ensure that the problem is fixed.
421	
422	- When reviewing why the maintenance pipeline for Data Workspace was missing old temporary tables that should be deleted I uncovered a logic issue where if the base table contained capital letters (which is poor database hygiene in the first place) then related temporary tables wouldn�t be deleted since their names would (correctly) be all lower case.
423	- https://github.com/uktrade/data-flow/pull/3226.
424	- I tweaked the script to look for the .lower() table name, and subsequently added unit tests to account for this scenario using pytest�s @mock.patch and mock.call.
425	- To restrict the ability for users to add derived pipelines creating tables with capital letters, I created a PR to tweak the regex validator in the Django form so this was no longer possible. I then added an appropriate unit test in a parameterised format to account for this scenario and test the correct corresponding error message would be shown:
426	- https://github.com/uktrade/data-workspace-frontend/pull/3187
427	- I resolve problems with pipelines on a daily basis. We�ve created a pipeline performance dashboard that allows us to effectively monitor whether our pipelines are operational, who they belong to and for how long they�ve been working/not working. On this basis I check for newly failing pipelines in airflow and troubleshoot the issue:
428	- Often an upstream dependency has failed and this requires a simple restart on both pipelines to fix them.
429	- Other times for example for SharePoint pipelines, the source URL/headers have changed which results in the pipeline not being able to ingest the pipeline. Contacting the author and retrieving up to date config information quickly fixes these issues.
430	- Credentials for APIs sometimes expire. In order to resolve these issues I contact the IAM/IAO for up to date credentials and update the secrets in vault so the pipeline can run again.
431	- Mobbing with Peter and Nona, we refactored a complicated pipeline which ingests Comtrade data into Data Workspace: https://github.com/uktrade/data-flow/pull/3347. 
432	- This involved optimising data types from generic VARCHARs to more appropriate data types. Crucially this refactor also involved troubleshooting sporadically missing data reported by our users. 
433	- There was a loss of data originating from some but not all large compressed input archives when being ingested. I identified the problem to be associated with the way the script was handling the decompression using gunzip/zlib libraries, since the output tables to these files were all 100,000 rows exactly. 
434	- Then through mobbing with senior engineers we identified that due to misconfigured compression of the input archives there were in fact multiple streams of data that we weren�t accounting for. Consequently we added in logic to check for multiple streams of data, and to decompress accordingly when there were unexpected end of file signals.
435	
436	Ensuring the appropriate actions to resolve problems
437	* I regularly resolve pipeline failures by quickly identifying the root causes of issues, whether they relate to data inconsistencies, system errors, or infrastructure conflicts.
438	* I�m adept at following code tracebacks and error codes to understand failures at the code level, and I use this information to determine the exact point of failure and next steps for resolution.
439	* This also requires myself to apply my knowledge of infrastructure interconnectivity, ensuring that I know where to look within the system to identify the source of the problem, whether it's within the database, the pipeline, or the orchestrator.
440	Coordinating teams to resolve problems
441	* Since leading the Data Pipeline Service (DPS) team I�ve guided junior engineers on a daily basis through the troubleshooting process, ensuring they not only fix issues but also understand how to identify and prevent similar problems in the future.
442	* I walk through debugging processes, such as addressing transactional locks in PostgreSQL, teaching the team how to go into RDS logs, identify the timestamp, and trace the SQL query that caused the issue.
443	* Facilitate cross-functional collaboration, ensuring that pipeline issues are resolved in a timely manner, and that everyone on the team understands the root causes of failures.
444	Implementing solutions and preventative measures
445	* In cases of transactional lock issues in PostgreSQL, I resolve conflicts by identifying which pipeline(s) caused the issue, deducing which SQL queries were responsible, and ensuring those pipelines are adjusted to prevent recurrence. For a recent example, the VacuumAuthPipeline which reindexes the database as one of its tasks, has been locking out our pipelines that ingest partitioned datasets which require specific constraints. I identified this connection using the above strategy, and implemented a solution that will reduce the likelihood of future locks.
446	* When building pipelines, I�ve learnt that its essential to implement preventative measures such as optimizing transaction handling in the database, improving pipeline schedules to reduce concurrency, and adding fail-safes for common errors, reducing the likelihood of failures in the future.
447	* When I teach my team problem solving techniques in our pipeline fixes, I always push for continuous improvements in pipeline design, logging, and monitoring, ensuring that future failures can be quickly identified and mitigated before they affect end-users.
448

### 6. NEW — `src/content/projects/grocery-lens.mdx`

New project page for Grocery Lens — Tom's current main project and the best showcase of his skills.

\```mdx
---
title: "Grocery Lens"
slug: "grocery-lens"
description: "A Django/PostgreSQL data product with custom dietary filters, recipe matching, basket synchronisation and NLP-assisted ingredient processing — built and operated from scratch."
date: "2025-07-01"
tags: ["Python", "Django", "PostgreSQL", "Docker", "Playwright", "spaCy", "Grafana"]
thumbnail: "/media/projects/grocery-lens/thumbnail.jpg"
featured: true
status: "in-progress"
---

## Overview

Grocery Lens is a Django/PostgreSQL data product I've been building from scratch since July 2025. It combines custom dietary filters, recipe matching, basket synchronisation and NLP-assisted ingredient processing to help users navigate grocery data in ways retailer apps don't support.

The backend is the core challenge: designing a platform that handles scraping, ingestion, data quality repair, search and analytics from the same PostgreSQL schema — without losing data integrity as the product grows.

## What I Built

- **Production data platform:** PostgreSQL schemas for semi-structured product and analytics workloads using `JSONField`, `ArrayField`, GIN indexes and integrity constraints
- **Replayable ingestion workflows:** loading retailer scrapes, public datasets and CSV logs with upserts, transactional writes, structured repair tables for relinking, backfills and taxonomy improvement
- **NLP ingredient processing:** canonicalisation and fuzzy matching with spaCy; fixed a scraper memory leak that reached 5.9 GB over 5,938 products
- **Concurrency-aware processing:** bounded concurrency, retry-safe queues using `select_for_update(skip_locked)` to balance throughput and reliability
- **Privacy-aware analytics:** retention controls, aggregation layers and reporting controls suitable for a live service
- **Observability:** Grafana/Loki log aggregation, health checks, blue/green deployments and rollback-safe traffic switching

## Technical Choices

- **Django ORM + raw SQL** — Django models for application logic; raw SQL for complex ingestion queries, upserts and repair workflows where the ORM gets in the way
- **PostgreSQL** — JSONField and ArrayField for semi-structured product data; GIN indexes for fast search; queue-backed processing patterns for background work
- **Playwright + BeautifulSoup** — Playwright for JS-rendered retailer pages; BeautifulSoup for lighter scraping tasks
- **Docker + Caddy** — containerised service behind Caddy, deployed blue/green with a health-check-gated swap script (same pattern as this portfolio)
- **Grafana + Loki** — structured log aggregation for pipeline health and data quality trend monitoring
\```

---

# Portfolio Personalisation — Content Update Plan (v3, final)

## Context

Thomas Swart (Tom), PhD. Data Engineer / Founder. Sources: `DE_CV_Upwork.pdf` and `Comaea DE - TS - 2025.txt` (DDAT evidence). The portfolio has been partially personalised by Tom. This plan covers remaining placeholder content, the Grocery Lens project page, the avatar photo, and a discovered production bug with `next/image` + volume-mounted media.

---

## What's already done (don't touch)

- `layout.tsx` — name, GitHub/LinkedIn URLs, locale `en_GB`, jobTitle
- `Hero.tsx` — name ("Tom Swart"), role ("Data Engineer")
- `Bio.tsx` — name, role, second paragraph (bouldering / LLMs)
- `Footer.tsx` — name

---

## Bug fix (affects production)

### `src/components/projects/ProjectCard.tsx` + `src/components/about/Bio.tsx` — add `unoptimized` to `<Image>`

**Problem:** `next/image` optimises images via `/_next/image?url=/media/...`, which makes an internal fetch to the Next.js server. But `/media/` files are served by Caddy and deleted from the Docker image — so the internal fetch gets a 404 in production. In dev (`npm run dev`) it works because `public/media/` exists on disk.

**Fix:** Add `unoptimized` prop to every `<Image>` that references a `/media/` path. This passes the URL straight to the `<img>` tag; the browser then fetches `/media/...` directly, which Caddy serves correctly.

`ProjectCard.tsx` — change:
\```tsx
<Image src={project.thumbnail} alt={project.title} fill className="object-cover object-center" />
\```
to:
\```tsx
<Image src={project.thumbnail} alt={project.title} fill unoptimized className="object-cover object-center" />
\```

`Bio.tsx` — same `unoptimized` prop on the avatar `<Image>` (see change #5 below).

---

## Content changes

### 1. `src/components/home/Hero.tsx` — bio blurb

**Current:** "I design & build reliable backend systems..."

**Proposed:**
> "I build data ingestion pipelines, Django/PostgreSQL platforms, and scraping workflows — turning messy, unreliable data into production-ready systems. Currently founding Grocery Lens and available for contract work."

---

**First paragraph — proposed:**
> "I'm a data engineer specialising in ingestion pipelines, Django/PostgreSQL platforms, and scraping workflows. I build production-minded systems — with retries, idempotent loads, observability and clear failure modes — for teams that need messy data turned into something usable. Currently the founder and lead engineer at **Grocery Lens**."

**Social links:** replace both placeholder URLs with:
- `https://github.com/tswart-res`
- `https://www.linkedin.com/in/tom-swart-a58053b7/`

---

### 3. `src/components/layout/Footer.tsx` — social links

Same real URLs as above (currently both are placeholder).

---

### 4. `src/components/about/SkillsGrid.tsx` — replace `skillCategories`

    name: "Core Stack",

\```

---

**Update Bio.tsx:**
- Add `import Image from "next/image"` at the top
- Replace the `<div>` containing the 👤 emoji with an `<Image>` — the parent `<div>` already has `overflow-hidden` but needs `relative` added for `fill` to work:
  \```tsx
  <div className="relative h-28 w-28 shrink-0 rounded-full border-2 border-[var(--accent)] overflow-hidden">
    <Image
      src="/media/about/avatar.png"
      alt="Tom Swart"
      fill
      unoptimized
      className="object-cover object-top"

### 7. NEW — `src/content/projects/grocery-lens.mdx`

**Logo (thumbnail):**
- Source: `/mnt/c/Users/liltimmy/My Drive/GL/Logo/Grocery Lens Logo/Grocery Lens Logo.svg`
- Copy to: `public/media/projects/grocery-lens/thumbnail.svg`
- The SVG has a solid `#f1feca` background fill (lime green) — renders cleanly as a fill thumbnail
- `thumbnail` field in frontmatter updated to `.svg`

**Live URL:** `https://grocerylens.co.uk`

**D3 tree visualiser link:** `https://grocerylens.co.uk/diet/4/low-fodmap-template?view=tree` — mentioned in content

\```mdx
---
title: "Grocery Lens"
slug: "grocery-lens"
description: "A Django/PostgreSQL data product with custom dietary filters, recipe matching, basket synchronisation and NLP-assisted ingredient processing — built and operated from scratch."
date: "2025-07-01"
tags: ["Python", "Django", "PostgreSQL", "Docker", "Playwright", "spaCy", "Grafana"]
thumbnail: "/media/projects/grocery-lens/thumbnail.svg"
liveUrl: "https://grocerylens.co.uk"
featured: true
status: "in-progress"
---

## Overview

Grocery Lens is a Django/PostgreSQL data product I've been building from scratch since July 2025. It combines custom dietary filters, recipe matching, basket synchronisation and NLP-assisted ingredient processing to help users navigate grocery data in ways retailer apps don't support.

The backend is the core challenge: designing a platform that handles scraping, ingestion, data quality repair, search and analytics from the same PostgreSQL schema — without losing data integrity as the product grows.

## What I Built

- **Production data platform:** PostgreSQL schemas for semi-structured product and analytics workloads using `JSONField`, `ArrayField`, GIN indexes and integrity constraints
- **Replayable ingestion workflows:** loading retailer scrapes, public datasets and CSV logs with upserts, transactional writes and structured repair tables for relinking, backfills and taxonomy improvement
- **NLP ingredient processing:** canonicalisation and fuzzy matching with spaCy; fixed a scraper memory leak that reached 5.9 GB over 5,938 products
- **Concurrency-aware processing:** bounded concurrency, retry-safe queues using `select_for_update(skip_locked)` to balance throughput and reliability
- **D3.js dietary tree visualiser:** an interactive ingredient hierarchy view for dietary filters — [see it live](https://grocerylens.co.uk/diet/4/low-fodmap-template?view=tree)
- **Privacy-aware analytics:** retention controls, aggregation layers and reporting controls built for a live service
- **Observability:** Grafana/Loki log aggregation, health checks, blue/green deployments and rollback-safe traffic switching

## Technical Choices

- **Django ORM + raw SQL** — Django models for application logic; raw SQL for complex ingestion queries, upserts and repair workflows where the ORM adds unnecessary overhead
- **PostgreSQL** — `JSONField` and `ArrayField` for semi-structured product data; GIN indexes for fast search; queue-backed processing using `select_for_update(skip_locked)` for background work
- **Playwright + BeautifulSoup** — Playwright for JS-rendered retailer pages; BeautifulSoup for lighter scraping tasks
- **Docker + Caddy** — containerised service behind Caddy, deployed blue/green with a health-check-gated swap script
- **Grafana + Loki** — structured log aggregation for pipeline health and data quality trend monitoring
\```

---

import Image from "next/image";
import AnimatedSection from "@/components/shared/AnimatedSection";

export default function Bio() {
  return (
    <AnimatedSection className="mb-16">
      <div className="flex flex-col gap-8 sm:flex-row sm:items-start sm:gap-12">
        {/* Avatar */}
        <div className="shrink-0">
          <div className="relative h-28 w-28 rounded-full border-2 border-[var(--accent)] overflow-hidden">
            <Image
              src="/media/about/avatar.png"
              alt="Tom Swart"
              fill
              unoptimized
              className="object-cover object-top"
            />
          </div>
        </div>

        <div>
          <h1 className="text-4xl font-bold text-[var(--fg)] mb-1">
            Tom Swart
          </h1>
          <p className="font-mono text-sm text-[var(--accent)] mb-4">
            Data Engineer
          </p>
          <div className="space-y-3 text-[var(--muted)] leading-relaxed max-w-prose">
            <p>
              I&apos;m a data engineer specialising in ingestion pipelines,
              Django/PostgreSQL platforms, and scraping workflows. I build
              production-minded systems — with retries, idempotent loads,
              observability and clear failure modes — for teams that need messy
              data turned into something usable. Currently the founder and lead
              engineer at{" "}
              <span className="text-[var(--fg)]">Grocery Lens</span>.
            </p>
            <p>
              When I&apos;m not writing code, I&apos;m tinkering with homelab
              setups, bouldering, or testing the latest local LLM released on
              HuggingFace.
            </p>
          </div>
          <div className="flex gap-4 mt-6 text-sm">
            <a
              href="https://github.com/tswart-res"
              target="_blank"
              rel="noopener noreferrer"
              className="text-[var(--muted)] hover:text-[var(--accent)] transition-colors font-mono"
            >
              github ↗
            </a>
            <a
              href="https://www.linkedin.com/in/tom-swart-a58053b7/"
              target="_blank"
              rel="noopener noreferrer"
              className="text-[var(--muted)] hover:text-[var(--accent)] transition-colors font-mono"
            >
              linkedin ↗
            </a>
          </div>
        </div>
      </div>
    </AnimatedSection>
  );
}

```

```mdx
---
title: "Grocery Lens"
slug: "grocery-lens"
description: "A Django/PostgreSQL data product with custom dietary filters, recipe matching, basket synchronisation and NLP-assisted ingredient processing — built and operated from scratch."
date: "2025-07-01"
tags: ["Python", "Django", "PostgreSQL", "Docker", "Playwright", "spaCy", "Grafana"]
thumbnail: "/media/projects/grocery-lens/thumbnail.svg"
liveUrl: "https://grocerylens.co.uk"
featured: true
status: "in-progress"
---

## Overview

Grocery Lens is a Django/PostgreSQL data product I've been building from scratch since July 2025. It combines custom dietary filters, recipe matching, basket synchronisation and NLP-assisted ingredient processing to help users navigate grocery data in ways retailer apps don't support.

The backend is the core challenge: designing a platform that handles scraping, ingestion, data quality repair, search and analytics from the same PostgreSQL schema — without losing data integrity as the product grows.

## What I Built

- **Production data platform:** PostgreSQL schemas for semi-structured product and analytics workloads using `JSONField`, `ArrayField`, GIN indexes and integrity constraints
- **Replayable ingestion workflows:** loading retailer scrapes, public datasets and CSV logs with upserts, transactional writes and structured repair tables for relinking, backfills and taxonomy improvement
- **NLP ingredient processing:** canonicalisation and fuzzy matching with spaCy; fixed a scraper memory leak that reached 5.9 GB over 5,938 products
- **Concurrency-aware processing:** bounded concurrency, retry-safe queues using `select_for_update(skip_locked)` to balance throughput and reliability
- **D3.js dietary tree visualiser:** an interactive ingredient hierarchy view for dietary filters — [see it live](https://grocerylens.co.uk/diet/4/low-fodmap-template?view=tree)
- **Privacy-aware analytics:** retention controls, aggregation layers and reporting controls built for a live service
- **Observability:** Grafana/Loki log aggregation, health checks, blue/green deployments and rollback-safe traffic switching

## Technical Choices

- **Django ORM + raw SQL** — Django models for application logic; raw SQL for complex ingestion queries, upserts and repair workflows where the ORM adds unnecessary overhead
- **PostgreSQL** — `JSONField` and `ArrayField` for semi-structured product data; GIN indexes for fast search; queue-backed processing using `select_for_update(skip_locked)` for background work
- **Playwright + BeautifulSoup** — Playwright for JS-rendered retailer pages; BeautifulSoup for lighter scraping tasks
- **Docker + Caddy** — containerised service behind Caddy, deployed blue/green with a health-check-gated swap script
- **Grafana + Loki** — structured log aggregation for pipeline health and data quality trend monitoring

```
