<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./infra/deploy.sh`

```text
1	#!/usr/bin/env bash
2	# deploy.sh — Zero-downtime blue/green deployment for the portfolio.
3	#
4	# Requires:
5	#   - Docker + docker compose (v2 plugin syntax)
6	#   - Caddy running as a systemd service, admin API on localhost:2019
7	#   - git repo initialised (used for image tagging)
8	#   - infra/Caddyfile.tmpl with __PORT__ placeholder
9	#   - .env.production in the portfolio root
10	#
11	# Usage: bash infra/deploy.sh [--dry-run]
12	#
13	# --dry-run prints every action but does not execute it.
14	
15	set -euo pipefail
16	
17	REPO_ROOT="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/.." && pwd)"
18	ACTIVE_PORT_FILE="$REPO_ROOT/infra/.active_port"
19	CADDYFILE_TMPL="$REPO_ROOT/infra/Caddyfile.tmpl"
20	CADDYFILE="$REPO_ROOT/infra/Caddyfile"
21	COMPOSE_FILE="$REPO_ROOT/docker/docker-compose.yml"
22	
23	HEALTH_MAX_RETRIES=15
24	HEALTH_RETRY_INTERVAL=3   # seconds
25	DRAIN_SECONDS=5
26	
27	DRY_RUN=false
28	[[ "${1:-}" == "--dry-run" ]] && DRY_RUN=true
29	
30	# ── Helpers ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
31	log() { echo "[deploy] $*"; }
32	
33	# run CMD [ARGS...] — execute or print in dry-run mode.
34	# Uses "$@" (array form, no eval) — safe with paths containing spaces.
35	run() {
36	  if [[ "$DRY_RUN" == "true" ]]; then
37	    echo "[dry-run]" "$@"
38	  else
39	    "$@"
40	  fi
41	}
42	
43	# ── Determine slots ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
44	if [[ -f "$ACTIVE_PORT_FILE" ]]; then
45	  CURRENT_PORT=$(cat "$ACTIVE_PORT_FILE")
46	else
47	  CURRENT_PORT=3001   # default: blue is active on first deploy
48	fi
49	
50	if [[ "$CURRENT_PORT" == "3001" ]]; then
51	  CURRENT_SLOT="blue"
52	  NEW_SLOT="green"
53	  NEW_PORT=3002
54	else
55	  CURRENT_SLOT="green"
56	  NEW_SLOT="blue"
57	  NEW_PORT=3001
58	fi
59	
60	log "Active slot : $CURRENT_SLOT (port $CURRENT_PORT)"
61	log "Target slot : $NEW_SLOT (port $NEW_PORT)"
62	
63	# ── Build image ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
64	GIT_HASH=$(git -C "$REPO_ROOT" rev-parse --short HEAD 2>/dev/null || echo "nogit")
65	TIMESTAMP=$(date +%s)
66	DEPLOY_TAG="${GIT_HASH}-${TIMESTAMP}"
67	IMAGE="portfolio:${DEPLOY_TAG}"
68	
69	log "Building image $IMAGE ..."
70	run docker build -f "$REPO_ROOT/docker/Dockerfile" -t "$IMAGE" "$REPO_ROOT"
71	
72	# ── Start new slot ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
73	log "Starting portfolio-$NEW_SLOT ..."
74	run env DEPLOY_TAG="$DEPLOY_TAG" docker compose -f "$COMPOSE_FILE" up -d "portfolio-$NEW_SLOT"
75	
76	# ── Health check ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
77	if [[ "$DRY_RUN" == "false" ]]; then
78	  log "Polling http://localhost:$NEW_PORT/api/health ..."
79	  HEALTHY=false
80	  for i in $(seq 1 "$HEALTH_MAX_RETRIES"); do
81	    if curl -sf "http://localhost:$NEW_PORT/api/health" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
82	      HEALTHY=true
83	      log "Health check passed on attempt $i."
84	      break
85	    fi
86	    log "Attempt $i/$HEALTH_MAX_RETRIES — not ready, retrying in ${HEALTH_RETRY_INTERVAL}s ..."
87	    sleep "$HEALTH_RETRY_INTERVAL"
88	  done
89	
90	  if [[ "$HEALTHY" == "false" ]]; then
91	    log "ERROR: Health check failed after $HEALTH_MAX_RETRIES attempts."
92	    log "Stopping portfolio-$NEW_SLOT. Old slot stays live."
93	    docker compose -f "$COMPOSE_FILE" stop "portfolio-$NEW_SLOT"
94	    exit 1
95	  fi
96	fi
97	
98	# ── Generate Caddyfile and reload ─────────────────────────────────────────────
99	# sed and the output redirect need to be done directly — shell redirection
100	# cannot be expressed as a "$@"-style command array.
101	log "Generating $CADDYFILE (port $NEW_PORT) ..."
102	if [[ "$DRY_RUN" == "true" ]]; then
103	  echo "[dry-run] sed 's/__PORT__/$NEW_PORT/' '$CADDYFILE_TMPL' > '$CADDYFILE'"
104	else
105	  sed "s/__PORT__/$NEW_PORT/" "$CADDYFILE_TMPL" > "$CADDYFILE"
106	fi
107	
108	log "Reloading Caddy ..."
109	run caddy reload --config "$CADDYFILE" --force
110	
111	# ── Save active state ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
112	log "Saving active port ..."
113	if [[ "$DRY_RUN" == "true" ]]; then
114	  echo "[dry-run] echo $NEW_PORT > '$ACTIVE_PORT_FILE'"
115	else
116	  echo "$NEW_PORT" > "$ACTIVE_PORT_FILE"
117	fi
118	log "Active port updated to $NEW_PORT."
119	
120	# ── Drain and stop old slot ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
121	log "Draining in-flight requests (${DRAIN_SECONDS}s) ..."
122	run sleep "$DRAIN_SECONDS"
123	
124	log "Stopping portfolio-$CURRENT_SLOT ..."
125	run docker compose -f "$COMPOSE_FILE" stop "portfolio-$CURRENT_SLOT"
126	
127	log "Deploy complete. Active: $NEW_SLOT (port $NEW_PORT), image: $IMAGE"
128
```
</details></tool-use>

Done. Three changes:

- **`Caddyfile.tmpl`** — `root * /home/debiantreeb/portfolio/public` → `root * __SITE_ROOT__`
- **`deploy.sh`** — sed now substitutes both placeholders: `s|__PORT__|$NEW_PORT|; s|__SITE_ROOT__|$REPO_ROOT/public|` using `|` as the delimiter (necessary since the path contains `/`). `REPO_ROOT` is already computed dynamically from the script's own location, so no username anywhere.
- **`cloudflared-config.yml`** — `debiantreeb` → `YOUR_USERNAME`, consistent with the other `_HERE` placeholders in that file.

The repo is now clean to push publicly.

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-04-14 15:09:43Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-04-14 15:09:55Z)**_

Yes, it will conflict. And yes, Dockerized Caddy+cloudflared is the right call. Here's the full picture:

---

**The conflict**

Your GL setup has Docker Caddy binding to host port `80`. The portfolio's system Caddy also wants port `80`. One will refuse to start — whichever launches second loses.

The `docker-compose.yml` also has a hardcoded `/home/debiantreeb/...` volume path that needs fixing regardless.

---

**Three options:**

**Option A — Quick fix: portfolio Caddy on a different host port**
Portfolio Caddy binds to `127.0.0.1:8080` instead of `80`. Cloudflared points to `localhost:8080`. GL is untouched. Minimal changes but slightly messy (non-standard port, system-level Caddy daemon still required for portfolio).

**Option B — Full Docker (recommended): internal network, no host port conflict**
Portfolio gets Caddy + cloudflared in the same Docker Compose. They communicate on a Docker-internal bridge network — cloudflared → caddy:80 → app containers — so nothing binds to host port `80` at all. No conflict with GL regardless of how GL is configured. Consistent with how GL is set up. No system Caddy daemon required.

The changes it requires:
- Add `caddy` and `cloudflared` services to `docker-compose.yml`
- Caddyfile.tmpl: media root becomes `/srv/media` (container path), backend target changes from `localhost:__PORT__` to `portfolio-blue:3000` / `portfolio-green:3000` (Docker service names)
- `deploy.sh`: `caddy reload` changes to `docker exec portfolio-caddy caddy reload ...`; health check stays on localhost since blue/green still expose `127.0.0.1:3001/3002` for that purpose only
- `cloudflared-config.yml`: service target becomes `http://caddy` (Docker service name)
- Volume paths change to relative (`../public/media`)

**Option C — Shared Caddy for both sites**
One Caddy instance routes `grocerylens.co.uk` and `portfolio.domain.com` by hostname. Tighter coupling — a bad portfolio deploy could reload the GL Caddy config too. Not recommended unless you want to manage them as one unit.

---

**My recommendation: Option B.** It's what you're already doing with GL, eliminates the conflict permanently, removes the system Caddy dependency, and makes the whole thing portable to a new server without any host-level setup.

Want me to implement Option B? It's a meaningful set of changes to `docker-compose.yml`, `deploy.sh`, `Caddyfile.tmpl`, and `cloudflared-config.yml` — I'll do it all in one go once you confirm.

---

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-04-14 15:11:33Z)**_

Let me read the remaining infra files before touching anything:

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-04-14 15:11:33Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./infra/caddy-setup.sh`

```text
1	#!/usr/bin/env bash
2	# caddy-setup.sh — One-time Caddy systemd setup.
3	#
4	# Run this once on first deployment to configure Caddy to use the
5	# portfolio's generated Caddyfile instead of the default /etc/caddy/Caddyfile.
6	#
7	# Subsequent deploys use: caddy reload --config infra/Caddyfile --force
8	
9	set -euo pipefail
10	
11	REPO_ROOT="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/.." && pwd)"
12	CADDYFILE_TMPL="$REPO_ROOT/infra/Caddyfile.tmpl"
13	CADDYFILE="$REPO_ROOT/infra/Caddyfile"
14	
15	echo "[setup] Generating initial Caddyfile (blue slot, port 3001) ..."
16	sed "s/__PORT__/3001/" "$CADDYFILE_TMPL" > "$CADDYFILE"
17	echo "3001" > "$REPO_ROOT/infra/.active_port"
18	
19	echo "[setup] Validating Caddyfile ..."
20	caddy validate --config "$CADDYFILE"
21	
22	echo "[setup] Installing systemd override to use portfolio Caddyfile ..."
23	sudo mkdir -p /etc/systemd/system/caddy.service.d/
24	sudo tee /etc/systemd/system/caddy.service.d/override.conf > /dev/null << EOF
25	[Service]
26	ExecStart=
27	ExecStart=/usr/bin/caddy run --environ --config $CADDYFILE
28	EOF
29	
30	echo "[setup] Reloading systemd and starting Caddy ..."
31	sudo systemctl daemon-reload
32	sudo systemctl enable --now caddy
33	
34	echo "[setup] Done. Caddy is now serving on port 80 from $CADDYFILE"
35	echo "[setup] To deploy: bash infra/deploy.sh"
36
```
</details></tool-use>

Good, now I have everything I need. Here's the full set of changes:

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
The Docker internal network (`bridge` driver) gives each service a DNS name matching its `container_name`. So `cloudflared` can reach Caddy at `http://caddy` and Caddy can reach Next.js at `portfolio-blue:3000` — no host port binding needed. The only ports exposed to the host are `127.0.0.1:3001/3002` so `deploy.sh` can curl health checks without a port conflict.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-04-14 15:13:26Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```yaml
# Blue/green deployment — caddy, cloudflared, and two app slots.
# All services share an internal bridge network; nothing binds to host port 80.
# deploy.sh manages which slot Caddy proxies to.
# DEPLOY_TAG is set by deploy.sh (e.g. abc1234-1713123456)

services:
  caddy:
    image: caddy:2-alpine
    container_name: portfolio-caddy
    restart: unless-stopped
    volumes:
      - ../infra/Caddyfile:/etc/caddy/Caddyfile:ro
      - ../public/media:/srv/media:ro
    networks:
      - internal

  cloudflared:
    image: cloudflare/cloudflared:latest
    container_name: portfolio-cloudflared
    restart: unless-stopped
    command: tunnel --config /etc/cloudflared/config.yml run
    volumes:
      - ../infra/cloudflared-config.yml:/etc/cloudflared/config.yml:ro
      # Place your tunnel credentials JSON in infra/.cloudflared/ (gitignored)
      - ../infra/.cloudflared:/etc/cloudflared/creds:ro
    networks:
      - internal
    depends_on:
      - caddy

  portfolio-blue:
    image: portfolio:${DEPLOY_TAG:-latest}
    container_name: portfolio-blue
    restart: unless-stopped
    # Exposed to localhost only — used by deploy.sh for health checks.
    # Caddy reaches this service via Docker DNS (portfolio-blue:3000), not this port.
    ports:
      - "127.0.0.1:3001:3000"
    volumes:
      - ../public/media:/app/public/media:ro
    env_file:
      - ../.env.production
    networks:
      - internal
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "wget -qO- http://localhost:3000/api/health || exit 1"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 3
      start_period: 15s

  portfolio-green:
    image: portfolio:${DEPLOY_TAG:-latest}
    container_name: portfolio-green
    restart: unless-stopped
    ports:
      - "127.0.0.1:3002:3000"
    volumes:
      - ../public/media:/app/public/media:ro
    env_file:
      - ../.env.production
    networks:
      - internal
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "wget -qO- http://localhost:3000/api/health || exit 1"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 3
      start_period: 15s

networks:
  internal:
    driver: bridge

```

```

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-04-14 15:13:33Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```tmpl
{
    # Admin API — used by "docker exec portfolio-caddy caddy reload"
    admin localhost:2019
}

:80 {
    # Serve media files directly — bypasses Next.js entirely.
    # Caddy handles HTTP range requests (206 Partial Content) for video seeking.
    handle /media/* {
        root * /srv/media
        file_server
    }

    # Reverse proxy to the active deployment slot.
    # __BACKEND__ is replaced with portfolio-blue:3000 or portfolio-green:3000
    # by deploy.sh via sed. Caddy reaches the slot via Docker internal DNS.
    handle {
        reverse_proxy __BACKEND__ {
            health_uri /api/health
            health_interval 30s
        }
    }

    # Security headers
    header {
        X-Frame-Options "SAMEORIGIN"
        X-Content-Type-Options "nosniff"
        Referrer-Policy "strict-origin-when-cross-origin"
        Permissions-Policy "camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=()"
        -Server
    }

    encode gzip zstd
}

```

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```yaml
# Cloudflare Tunnel configuration template.
#
# Setup steps (when you have a domain):
#   1. cloudflared tunnel create portfolio
#   2. cloudflared tunnel route dns portfolio your-domain.com
#   3. cloudflared tunnel route dns portfolio www.your-domain.com
#   4. Replace TUNNEL_ID_HERE with the UUID from step 1
#   5. Copy the credentials JSON to infra/.cloudflared/TUNNEL_ID_HERE.json
#      (this directory is gitignored — credentials are never committed)
#
# Traffic flow: Cloudflare → cloudflared → caddy (Docker internal DNS) → Next.js
# Caddy handles all TLS — the tunnel connection is plain HTTP internally.

tunnel: TUNNEL_ID_HERE
credentials-file: /etc/cloudflared/creds/TUNNEL_ID_HERE.json

ingress:
  - hostname: your-domain.com
    service: http://caddy
  - hostname: www.your-domain.com
    service: http://caddy
  # Catch-all — required by cloudflared
  - service: http_status:404

```

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-04-14 15:14:03Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```sh
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# caddy-setup.sh — One-time first-run setup for a fresh server.
#
# Replaces the old systemd Caddy setup — Caddy now runs as a Docker container.
#
# Prerequisites before running this:
#   1. Docker + docker compose (v2 plugin syntax) installed
#   2. .env.production present in the portfolio root
#   3. Tunnel credentials JSON in infra/.cloudflared/TUNNEL_ID.json
#      (create tunnel: cloudflared tunnel create portfolio)
#   4. infra/cloudflared-config.yml updated with your TUNNEL_ID and domain
#
# After this script: use "bash infra/deploy.sh" for all future deploys.

set -euo pipefail

REPO_ROOT="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")/.." && pwd)"
COMPOSE_FILE="$REPO_ROOT/docker/docker-compose.yml"
CADDYFILE_TMPL="$REPO_ROOT/infra/Caddyfile.tmpl"
CADDYFILE="$REPO_ROOT/infra/Caddyfile"
ACTIVE_PORT_FILE="$REPO_ROOT/infra/.active_port"

# Ensure credentials directory exists (contents are gitignored)
mkdir -p "$REPO_ROOT/infra/.cloudflared"

echo "[setup] Generating initial Caddyfile (blue slot) ..."
sed "s|__BACKEND__|portfolio-blue:3000|" "$CADDYFILE_TMPL" > "$CADDYFILE"
echo "3001" > "$ACTIVE_PORT_FILE"

echo "[setup] Building initial Docker image ..."
docker build -f "$REPO_ROOT/docker/Dockerfile" -t "portfolio:latest" "$REPO_ROOT"

echo "[setup] Starting all services (caddy, cloudflared, portfolio-blue) ..."
docker compose -f "$COMPOSE_FILE" up -d

echo ""
echo "[setup] Done. Services running:"
docker compose -f "$COMPOSE_FILE" ps
echo ""
echo "[setup] To deploy updates: bash infra/deploy.sh"

```

```

Clean. Here's a summary of every change and the reasoning:

---

**`docker/docker-compose.yml`** — complete rewrite
- Added `caddy` service (image `caddy:2-alpine`), mounts `infra/Caddyfile` and `public/media` (relative paths, no username anywhere)
- Added `cloudflared` service, mounts config and `infra/.cloudflared/` creds dir
- `portfolio-blue/green`: removed host paths, use `../public/media` relative path; expose only `127.0.0.1:3001/3002` to localhost (health checks only)
- All services on a single `internal` bridge network — nothing binds to host port 80

**`infra/Caddyfile.tmpl`**
- `root * /srv/media` (container path, fixed — no more `__SITE_ROOT__` placeholder)
- `reverse_proxy __BACKEND__` — replaced by `portfolio-blue:3000` or `portfolio-green:3000` via Docker DNS

**`infra/deploy.sh`**
- sed: `s|__BACKEND__|portfolio-$NEW_SLOT:3000|` (was `__PORT__` + `__SITE_ROOT__`)
- Caddy reload: `docker exec portfolio-caddy caddy reload --config /etc/caddy/Caddyfile --force` (was system `caddy reload`)
- Health check curl to `localhost:3001/3002` unchanged — still works via the localhost-only exposed ports

**`infra/cloudflared-config.yml`**
- `credentials-file` now points to `/etc/cloudflared/creds/` (the container-internal mount)
- Service target: `http://caddy` (Docker DNS) instead of `localhost`

**`infra/caddy-setup.sh`** — rewritten as Docker first-run script (no more systemd)

**`.gitignore` + `infra/.cloudflared/.gitkeep`** — creds dir tracked but its contents ignored, same pattern as `public/media/`

---

**When you're ready to deploy to the server**, the steps are:
1. Copy tunnel credentials to `infra/.cloudflared/TUNNEL_ID.json`
2. Update `infra/cloudflared-config.yml` with your tunnel ID and domain
3. `bash infra/caddy-setup.sh` — first time only
4. `bash infra/deploy.sh` — every update after that

---

 `./README.md`

```text
1	# Portfolio
2	
3	A self-hosted Data Engineer portfolio built with Next.js 16, Tailwind CSS v4, and MDX-driven project pages. Runs in Docker behind Caddy as a reverse proxy, deployed via a blue/green shell script with zero downtime.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Table of Contents
8	
9	1. [Local Development](#1-local-development)
10	2. [Environment Variables](#2-environment-variables)
11	3. [Adding a Project](#3-adding-a-project)
12	4. [Production Deployment](#4-production-deployment)
13	   - [Prerequisites](#41-prerequisites)
14	   - [First-time Caddy Setup](#42-first-time-caddy-setup)
15	   - [Deploy](#43-deploy)
16	5. [Cloudflare Tunnel](#5-cloudflare-tunnel)
17	6. [Media Files](#6-media-files)
18	7. [Tech Stack](#7-tech-stack)
19	
20	---
21	
22	## 1. Local Development
23	
24	```bash
25	# 1. Install dependencies
26	npm install
27	
28	# 2. Create a local env file
29	cp .env.example .env.local
30	# Edit .env.local — set EMAIL_ENABLED=false to skip Resend during dev
31	
32	# 3. Start the dev server
33	npm run dev
34	# → http://localhost:3000
35	```
36	
37	To test the exact production Docker image locally:
38	
39	```bash
40	docker build -f docker/Dockerfile -t portfolio:local .
41	
42	docker run --rm -p 3000:3000 \
43	  -e EMAIL_ENABLED=false \
44	  -e NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL=http://localhost:3000 \
45	  portfolio:local
46	# → http://localhost:3000
47	```
48	
49	---
50	
51	## 2. Environment Variables
52	
53	Copy `.env.example` to `.env.production` before deploying.
54	
55	| Variable | Required | Description |
56	|---|---|---|
57	| `RESEND_API_KEY` | Yes (if `EMAIL_ENABLED=true`) | API key from [resend.com](https://resend.com) |
58	| `RESEND_FROM_EMAIL` | Yes | Verified sender address in your Resend account |
59	| `RESEND_TO_EMAIL` | Yes | Your inbox — where contact form messages are delivered |
60	| `EMAIL_ENABLED` | No | Set to `false` to log instead of sending email (default: `true`) |
61	| `NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL` | Yes | Your public domain, no trailing slash (e.g. `https://you.com`) |
62	
63	```bash
64	cp .env.example .env.production
65	# Fill in the values
66	```
67	
68	`.env.production` is loaded by `docker-compose.yml` at runtime and is gitignored — never commit it.
69	
70	---
71	
72	## 3. Adding a Project
73	
74	Use the scaffold script:
75	
76	```bash
77	bash scripts/new-project.sh
78	```
79	
80	It will prompt for title, tags, URLs, etc. and create:
81	- `src/content/projects/<slug>.mdx` — pre-filled frontmatter + placeholder body
82	- `public/media/projects/<slug>/` — media directory for this project
83	
84	Then:
85	1. Edit the MDX file with your project description
86	2. Add a thumbnail: `cp your-image.jpg public/media/projects/<slug>/thumbnail.jpg`
87	3. Optionally add a video: `cp demo.mp4 public/media/projects/<slug>/demo.mp4`
88	
89	### Frontmatter reference
90	
91	```yaml
92	---
93	title: "My Project"
94	slug: "my-project"
95	description: "1–2 sentence card description."
96	date: "2025-06-01"
97	tags: ["Docker", "TypeScript"]
98	thumbnail: "/media/projects/my-project/thumbnail.jpg"
99	videoDemo: "/media/projects/my-project/demo.mp4"  # optional
100	repoUrl: "https://github.com/you/repo"             # optional
101	liveUrl: "https://example.com"                     # optional
102	featured: true        # shows on home page
103	status: "completed"   # "completed" | "in-progress" | "archived"
104	---
105	```
106	
107	---
108	
109	## 4. Production Deployment
110	
111	### 4.1 Prerequisites
112	
113	On the host machine you need:
114	
115	- **Docker** + the **compose** plugin (`docker compose` v2 syntax)
116	- **Caddy** installed as a systemd service (`sudo apt install caddy` or from [caddyserver.com](https://caddyserver.com/docs/install))
117	- **curl** (for health checks in `deploy.sh`)
118	- **git** initialised in this directory (the deploy script uses `git rev-parse --short HEAD` to tag images)
119	- `.env.production` filled in (see [§2](#2-environment-variables))
120	
121	```bash
122	# Initialise git if you haven't already
123	git init && git add . && git commit -m "init"
124	```
125	
126	### 4.2 First-time Caddy Setup
127	
128	Run this once to point Caddy's systemd service at the portfolio's generated Caddyfile:
129	
130	```bash
131	bash infra/caddy-setup.sh
132	```
133	
134	What it does:
135	1. Generates `infra/Caddyfile` from `infra/Caddyfile.tmpl` (blue slot, port 3001)
136	2. Validates the Caddyfile
137	3. Installs a systemd drop-in override so Caddy uses this file instead of `/etc/caddy/Caddyfile`
138	4. Enables and starts the Caddy service
139	
140	Verify Caddy is running:
141	```bash
142	sudo systemctl status caddy
143	curl http://localhost/api/health   # should return {"status":"ok"}
144	```
145	
146	### 4.3 Deploy
147	
148	```bash
149	bash infra/deploy.sh
150	```
151	
152	The script:
153	1. Reads the active slot from `infra/.active_port` (default: blue on 3001)
154	2. Builds a new Docker image tagged with the git commit SHA + timestamp
155	3. Starts the new (inactive) slot
156	4. Polls `/api/health` up to 15 times (3s apart) — if it never passes, tears down the new container and exits, leaving the old slot live
157	5. Generates a new `infra/Caddyfile` pointing at the new port and runs `caddy reload`
158	6. Waits 5 seconds for in-flight requests to drain
159	7. Stops the old slot
160	
161	Dry-run (prints actions without executing):
162	```bash
163	bash infra/deploy.sh --dry-run
164	```
165	
166	### Caddy config notes
167	
168	`infra/Caddyfile.tmpl` is committed to git. `infra/Caddyfile` (the generated active config) is gitignored.
169	
170	- `/media/*` requests are served directly by Caddy from `public/media/` — this gives native HTTP range request support (206 Partial Content) for video seeking without involving Node.js.
171	- All other requests are reverse-proxied to the active slot.
172	
173	**If you later switch from Cloudflare Tunnel to direct DNS:** replace `:80` in `Caddyfile.tmpl` with your domain (e.g. `you.com`) and Caddy will auto-provision TLS via Let's Encrypt. These two modes are mutually exclusive — pick one.
174	
175	---
176	
177	## 5. Cloudflare Tunnel
178	
179	The tunnel routes internet traffic to Caddy on `localhost:80`. No ports need to be opened on your router.
180	
181	**When you have a domain:**
182	
183	```bash
184	# 1. Create the tunnel
185	cloudflared tunnel create portfolio
186	
187	# 2. Route your domain through it
188	cloudflared tunnel route dns portfolio your-domain.com
189	cloudflared tunnel route dns portfolio www.your-domain.com
190	
191	# 3. Fill in the tunnel ID in the config
192	#    Replace TUNNEL_ID_HERE in infra/cloudflared-config.yml
193	#    and copy the credentials JSON from ~/.cloudflared/ to the path in that file
194	
195	# 4. Run as a systemd service
196	sudo cloudflared service install
197	sudo systemctl enable --now cloudflared
198	```
199	
200	---
201	
202	## 6. Media Files
203	
204	Media is stored on the host at `public/media/` and bind-mounted read-only into the container. It is gitignored (only the directory skeleton is tracked).
205	
206	```
207	public/media/
208	└── projects/
209	    └── <slug>/
210	        ├── thumbnail.jpg   # used as project card image and MDX hero
211	        └── demo.mp4        # optional — referenced via videoDemo in frontmatter
212	```
213	
214	To add media without redeploying:
215	```bash
216	cp image.jpg ~/portfolio/public/media/projects/<slug>/thumbnail.jpg
217	# Caddy serves it immediately — no container restart needed
218	```
219	
220	Recommended sizes:
221	- Thumbnail: `1280×720` JPEG, compressed to under 200 KB
222	- Video: H.264 MP4, web-optimised (`ffmpeg -movflags +faststart`)
223	
224	---
225	
226	## 7. Tech Stack
227	
228	| Layer | Technology |
229	|---|---|
230	| Framework | Next.js 16 (App Router, TypeScript, `output: "standalone"`) |
231	| Styling | Tailwind CSS v4 (CSS-first, `@theme` tokens, Lightning CSS) |
232	| Animations | Motion v12 (import from `motion/react`) |
233	| Content | MDX via `next-mdx-remote/rsc` + `gray-matter` |
234	| Forms | `react-hook-form` + Zod v4 + `@hookform/resolvers@5` |
235	| Email | Resend |
236	| Container | Docker multi-stage, node:24-alpine, non-root user |
237	| Proxy | Caddy (host, systemd, `caddy reload` for zero-downtime swaps) |
238	| Tunnel | Cloudflare Tunnel (no open ports) |
239	| Deployment | Blue/green via `infra/deploy.sh` |
240
```
</details></tool-use>

/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens.mdx:4:description: "A Django/PostgreSQL data product with custom dietary filters, recipe matching, basket synchronisation and NLP-assisted ingredient processing — built and operated from scratch."
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens.mdx:17:The backend is the core challenge: designing a platform that handles scraping, ingestion, data quality repair, search and analytics from the same PostgreSQL schema — without losing data integrity as the product grows.
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens.mdx:21:- **Dietary filters** — user-created diet templates with ingredient-level allow/restrict rules; publicly posted diets support community upvoting and downvoting
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens.mdx:22:- **Recipe matching** — compatible recipes surfaced per diet; background caching pre-computes matches after a diet is saved so results are instant on first load
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens.mdx:23:- **Basket & favourites** — add products to a grocery basket, favourite individual items, track changes over time
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens.mdx:24:- **Price history** — per-product price tracking across scrape runs; surfaced in product detail and the compare page
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens.mdx:25:- **Compare page** — side-by-side product comparison across retailers
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens.mdx:26:- **D3.js dietary tree visualiser** — interactive ingredient hierarchy view — [see it live](https://grocerylens.co.uk/diet/4/low-fodmap-template/?view=tree)
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens.mdx:40:- **Django ORM + raw SQL** — Django models for application logic; raw SQL for complex ingestion queries, upserts and repair workflows where the ORM adds unnecessary overhead
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens.mdx:41:- **PostgreSQL** — `JSONField` and `ArrayField` for semi-structured product data; GIN indexes for fast search; queue-backed processing using `select_for_update(skip_locked)` for background work
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens.mdx:42:- **Playwright + BeautifulSoup** — Playwright for JS-rendered retailer pages; BeautifulSoup for lighter scraping tasks
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens.mdx:43:- **Docker + Caddy** — containerised service behind Caddy, deployed blue/green with a health-check-gated swap script
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens.mdx:44:- **Grafana + Loki** — structured log aggregation for pipeline health and data quality trend monitoring
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/portfolio-website.mdx:20:- **Tailwind CSS v4** with CSS-first configuration — no `tailwind.config.ts` needed
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/portfolio-website.mdx:27:Runs in Docker on a personal Linux machine, served through Caddy as a reverse proxy. Blue/green deployments via a `deploy.sh` script — zero downtime when pushing updates.
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens-diet-tree.mdx:4:description: "An interactive D3.js collapsible tree for navigating ingredient taxonomy and dietary filter hierarchies within Grocery Lens — with zoom/pan, animated transitions, and fullscreen support."
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens-diet-tree.mdx:15:Part of [Grocery Lens](/projects/grocery-lens). The dietary filter system models ingredient taxonomy as a tree — a diet like Low-FODMAP is the root, its rules form the branches, and individual ingredients are leaves. This visualiser makes that structure navigable directly in the browser rather than through flat filter lists.
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens-diet-tree.mdx:19:- **Collapsible D3 tree** — nodes expand and collapse on click; `d3.hierarchy` structures the data, `d3.tree` computes positions, animated SVG transitions smooth the reflow
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens-diet-tree.mdx:20:- **Zoom and pan** — full `d3.zoom` integration; users can freely navigate large taxonomies without losing context; a reset button snaps back to the default view
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens-diet-tree.mdx:21:- **Lazy loading** — the tree only fetches data when the Tree View tab is activated, avoiding an unnecessary API call on page load
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens-diet-tree.mdx:22:- **Fullscreen mode** — browser Fullscreen API toggled via a Feather icon button; the SVG reflows to fill the viewport
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens-diet-tree.mdx:23:- **Django REST endpoint** — a dedicated view at `/api/diet/<diet_id>/ingredient-hierarchy/` serialises the full ingredient subtree as nested JSON for a given diet template
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens-diet-tree.mdx:24:- **End-to-end tests** — Playwright + Django TestCase covering tab switching, tree rendering, lazy-load timing, fullscreen toggle, and API response shape
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens-diet-tree.mdx:28:- **D3.js** — `d3.hierarchy`, `d3.tree`, `d3.zoom`; SVG `<path>` elements with cubic Bezier curves for links; enter/update/exit transitions for smooth node animations
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens-diet-tree.mdx:29:- **Vanilla JS (`diet_tree_visualization.js`)** — no framework; the Django template embeds the serialised tree data and a single script initialises D3 against it
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens-diet-tree.mdx:30:- **Bootstrap 5** — tabs, buttons, alerts, and layout scaffolding; CSS custom property theme variables used for colour consistency with the rest of the Django site
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens-diet-tree.mdx:31:- **Feather icons** — tab icon, fullscreen toggle, and reset button; lightweight SVG sprite, no icon font dependency
/home/debiantreeb/portfolio/src/content/projects/grocery-lens-diet-tree.mdx:32:- **Playwright** — end-to-end test suite validates the full user flow: tab click → API fetch → SVG render → fullscreen → collapse/expand

 `./src/content/projects/homelab-setup.mdx`

```text
1	---
2	title: "Self-Hosted Homelab Setup"
3	slug: "homelab-setup"
4	description: "A fully self-hosted personal infrastructure — Caddy, Docker, Cloudflare tunnels, and blue/green deployments on a single Linux machine."
5	date: "2025-11-01"
6	tags: ["Docker", "Caddy", "Linux", "Cloudflare", "Self-hosted"]
7	thumbnail: "/media/projects/homelab-setup/thumbnail.jpg"
8	featured: true
9	status: "completed"
10	---
11	
12	## Overview
13	
14	This project documents my self-hosted homelab setup — everything from the base OS config to automated blue/green deployments and Cloudflare Tunnel integration.
15	
16	## What I Built
17	
18	- **Caddy** as the reverse proxy with automatic TLS via Cloudflare tunnels
19	- **Docker** containerisation for all services
20	- **Blue/Green deployment** via a shell script that health-checks the new container before swapping Caddy's upstream
21	- **Cloudflared** tunnel for zero-exposure ingress — no open ports on the router
22	
23	## Architecture
24	
25	The deployment pipeline looks like this:
26	
27	1. Run `deploy.sh` locally
28	2. Docker builds a new image tagged with the git commit SHA
29	3. The new container starts on the inactive port (3001 or 3002)
30	4. Health check passes → Caddy's config is regenerated with `sed` and reloaded
31	5. Old container is stopped after a 5-second drain window
32	
33	## Lessons Learned
34	
35	The biggest surprise was how reliable Caddy is for this use case — zero SSL configuration needed, and the `caddy reload` command atomically swaps configurations without dropping connections.
36
```
</details></tool-use>

```md
# Portfolio

A self-hosted Data Engineer portfolio built with Next.js 16, Tailwind CSS v4, and MDX-driven project pages. Runs fully in Docker (Next.js, Caddy, cloudflared) with zero-downtime blue/green deploys via a shell script.

---

## Table of Contents

1. [Local Development](#1-local-development)
2. [Environment Variables](#2-environment-variables)
3. [Adding a Project](#3-adding-a-project)
4. [Production Deployment](#4-production-deployment)
   - [Prerequisites](#41-prerequisites)
   - [First-time Setup](#42-first-time-setup)
   - [Deploy](#43-deploy)
5. [Cloudflare Tunnel](#5-cloudflare-tunnel)
6. [Media Files](#6-media-files)
7. [Tech Stack](#7-tech-stack)

---

## 1. Local Development

\```bash
# 1. Install dependencies
npm install

# 2. Create a local env file
cp .env.example .env.local
# Edit .env.local - set EMAIL_ENABLED=false to skip Resend during dev

# 3. Start the dev server
npm run dev
# http://localhost:3000
\```

To test the exact production Docker image locally:

\```bash
docker build -f docker/Dockerfile -t portfolio:local .

docker run --rm -p 3000:3000 \
  -e EMAIL_ENABLED=false \
  -e NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL=http://localhost:3000 \
  portfolio:local
# http://localhost:3000
\```

---

## 2. Environment Variables

Copy `.env.example` to `.env.production` before deploying.

| Variable | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `RESEND_API_KEY` | Yes (if `EMAIL_ENABLED=true`) | API key from [resend.com](https://resend.com) |
| `RESEND_FROM_EMAIL` | Yes | Verified sender address in your Resend account |
| `RESEND_TO_EMAIL` | Yes | Your inbox - where contact form messages land |
| `EMAIL_ENABLED` | No | Set to `false` to log instead of sending (default: `true`) |
| `NEXT_PUBLIC_SITE_URL` | Yes | Your public domain, no trailing slash (e.g. `https://you.com`) |

\```bash
cp .env.example .env.production
# Fill in the values
\```

`.env.production` is loaded by `docker-compose.yml` at runtime and is gitignored.

---

## 3. Adding a Project

Use the scaffold script:

\```bash
bash scripts/new-project.sh
\```

It prompts for title, tags, URLs, etc. and creates:
- `src/content/projects/<slug>.mdx` - pre-filled frontmatter and placeholder body
- `public/media/projects/<slug>/` - media directory for the project

Then:
1. Edit the MDX file with your project description
2. Add a thumbnail: `cp your-image.jpg public/media/projects/<slug>/thumbnail.jpg`
3. Optionally add a video: `cp demo.mp4 public/media/projects/<slug>/demo.mp4`

### Frontmatter reference

\```yaml
---
title: "My Project"
slug: "my-project"
description: "1-2 sentence card description."
date: "2025-06-01"
tags: ["Docker", "TypeScript"]
thumbnail: "/media/projects/my-project/thumbnail.jpg"
videoDemo: "/media/projects/my-project/demo.mp4"  # optional
repoUrl: "https://github.com/you/repo"             # optional
liveUrl: "https://example.com"                     # optional
featured: true        # shows on home page
status: "completed"   # "completed" | "in-progress" | "archived"
---
\```

---

## 4. Production Deployment

### 4.1 Prerequisites

- **Docker** + the **compose** plugin (`docker compose` v2 syntax)
- **curl** (for health checks in `deploy.sh`)
- **git** initialised in this directory (used to tag Docker images)
- `.env.production` filled in (see [§2](#2-environment-variables))
- Cloudflare Tunnel credentials in `infra/.cloudflared/` (see [§5](#5-cloudflare-tunnel))

\```bash
# Initialise git if you haven't already
git init && git add . && git commit -m "init"
\```

No system-level Caddy install needed - it runs as a Docker container.

### 4.2 First-time Setup

Run once on a fresh server to build the image and start all services:

\```bash
bash infra/caddy-setup.sh
\```

What it does:
1. Generates `infra/Caddyfile` from `infra/Caddyfile.tmpl` (blue slot)
2. Builds the initial `portfolio:latest` Docker image
3. Starts all services: `caddy`, `cloudflared`, `portfolio-blue`

Verify everything is up:
\```bash
docker compose -f docker/docker-compose.yml ps
curl http://localhost:3001/api/health   # should return {"status":"ok"}
\```

### 4.3 Deploy

\```bash
bash infra/deploy.sh
\```

The script:
1. Reads the active slot from `infra/.active_port` (default: blue on 3001)
2. Builds a new Docker image tagged with the git commit SHA + timestamp
3. Starts the new (inactive) slot
4. Polls `/api/health` up to 15 times (3s apart) - if it never passes, tears down the new container and exits, leaving the old slot live
5. Generates a new `infra/Caddyfile` pointing at the new slot and reloads Caddy via `docker exec portfolio-caddy caddy reload`
6. Waits 5 seconds to drain in-flight requests
7. Stops the old slot

Dry run (prints every action without executing):
\```bash
bash infra/deploy.sh --dry-run
\```

### Caddy config notes

`infra/Caddyfile.tmpl` is committed to git. `infra/Caddyfile` (the generated live config) is gitignored.

- `/media/*` requests are served directly by Caddy from `/srv/media` inside the container (bind-mounted from `public/media/`). This gives native HTTP range request support (206) for video seeking without involving Next.js.
- All other requests are reverse-proxied to the active slot via Docker internal DNS (`portfolio-blue:3000` or `portfolio-green:3000`).

**Switching from Cloudflare Tunnel to direct DNS:** replace `:80` in `Caddyfile.tmpl` with your domain (e.g. `you.com`) and Caddy will provision TLS via Let's Encrypt. The two modes are mutually exclusive.

---

## 5. Cloudflare Tunnel

The tunnel routes internet traffic to the Caddy container on the internal Docker network. No ports need to be opened on your router.

All services run in the same Docker Compose network, so cloudflared reaches Caddy at `http://caddy` (Docker internal DNS).

### Setup

\```bash
# 1. Install cloudflared locally (for tunnel setup only)
#    https://developers.cloudflare.com/cloudflared/get-started/

# 2. Log in and create the tunnel
cloudflared tunnel login
cloudflared tunnel create portfolio

# 3. Route your domain through it
cloudflared tunnel route dns portfolio your-domain.com
cloudflared tunnel route dns portfolio www.your-domain.com

# 4. Copy the credentials JSON into the project
#    The file will be at ~/.cloudflared/<TUNNEL_ID>.json
cp ~/.cloudflared/<TUNNEL_ID>.json infra/.cloudflared/<TUNNEL_ID>.json
# infra/.cloudflared/ is gitignored - credentials are never committed

# 5. Update infra/cloudflared-config.yml
#    Replace TUNNEL_ID_HERE with your tunnel UUID

# 6. Start (or restart) the cloudflared container
docker compose -f docker/docker-compose.yml up -d cloudflared
\```

---

## 6. Media Files

Media is stored at `public/media/` and bind-mounted read-only into both the Caddy container (`/srv/media`) and the Next.js containers (`/app/public/media`). The directory skeleton is tracked in git; the contents are gitignored.

\```
public/media/
├── about/
│   └── avatar.png
└── projects/
    └── <slug>/
        ├── thumbnail.jpg   # project card image and MDX hero
        └── demo.mp4        # optional - referenced via videoDemo in frontmatter
\```

To add media without redeploying:
\```bash
cp image.jpg ~/portfolio/public/media/projects/<slug>/thumbnail.jpg
# Caddy serves it immediately - no container restart needed
\```

Recommended sizes:
- Thumbnail: `1280x720` JPEG, under 200 KB
- Video: H.264 MP4, web-optimised (`ffmpeg -movflags +faststart`)

---

## 7. Tech Stack

| Layer | Technology |
|---|---|
| Framework | Next.js 16 (App Router, TypeScript, `output: "standalone"`) |
| Styling | Tailwind CSS v4 (CSS-first, `@theme` tokens, Lightning CSS) |
| Animations | Motion v12 (import from `motion/react`) |
| Content | MDX via `next-mdx-remote/rsc` + `gray-matter` |
| Forms | `react-hook-form` + Zod v4 + `@hookform/resolvers@5` |
| Email | Resend |
| Container | Docker multi-stage, node:24-alpine, non-root user |
| Proxy | Caddy (Docker container, `docker exec` reload for zero-downtime swaps) |
| Tunnel | Cloudflare Tunnel via cloudflared Docker container |
| Deployment | Blue/green via `infra/deploy.sh` |
